background_cases: A man strikes another, who falls and breaks his arm. The blow caused the injury.
A driver runs a red light and collides with a pedestrian. The driving caused the harm.
A company dumps chemicals into a river, and downstream residents fall ill. The dumping caused the illness.
definition: An event A is the cause of an event B if and only if (i) A occurred before B, (ii) but for A, B would not have occurred, and (iii) A and B are connected by a continuous chain of events.
conditions: A before B
but for A, B would not have occurred
continuous chain of events
counterexample: Two hunters independently and simultaneously fire at a victim. Either shot alone would have been fatal. But for hunter A's shot, the victim would still have died (from hunter B's shot), and vice versa. The but-for condition fails for each hunter, yet each is plainly a cause of the death.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved a single sufficient cause. The definition's but-for test works when there is one cause but fails when there are multiple independently sufficient causes — a case the background examples did not include.
passage:
Consider three cases of causation in law. A man strikes another, who falls and breaks his arm; the blow caused the injury. A driver runs a red light and collides with a pedestrian; the driving caused the harm. A company dumps chemicals into a river, and downstream residents fall ill; the dumping caused the illness. From these cases one might extract a definition: an event A is the cause of an event B if and only if A occurred before B, but for A, B would not have occurred, and A and B are connected by a continuous chain of events.

But this analysis falters in cases of overdetermination. Suppose two hunters, acting independently, fire at the same victim simultaneously. Either shot alone would have been fatal. The victim dies. Now apply the but-for test to hunter A: but for his shot, would the victim have died? Yes — from hunter B's shot. So the but-for condition is not satisfied. The same holds for hunter B. The but-for test exonerates both hunters, which is absurd — each plainly caused the death. The background cases all involved a single sufficient cause, and the but-for test works perfectly in such cases. The definition failed to anticipate situations where multiple independently sufficient causes converge, producing a result that each alone would have brought about.

background_cases: A patient agrees to surgery after the surgeon explains the risks; the consent is valid.
A customer signs a lease after reading and understanding the terms; the consent is valid.
A volunteer agrees to participate in a study after being briefed on the procedures; the consent is valid.
definition: Consent is legally valid if and only if (i) it is given voluntarily, (ii) the consenting party understands what he is consenting to, and (iii) the consent is expressed unambiguously.
conditions: given voluntarily
party understands
expressed unambiguously
counterexample: A consumer voluntarily signs a contract whose terms he fully understands, expressing his consent unambiguously. But the terms are so complex and one-sided that no reasonable person would agree to them if they were clearly presented. The three conditions are met, yet the consent seems vitiated by the gross inequality of the terms.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved agreements whose terms were substantively fair. The definition attended only to the process of consenting (voluntariness, understanding, expression) and dropped the substantive fairness that characterised the motivating cases.
passage:
Consider three cases of valid consent. A patient agrees to surgery after the surgeon explains the risks; the consent is valid. A customer signs a lease after reading and understanding the terms; the consent is valid. A volunteer agrees to participate in a study after being briefed on the procedures; the consent is valid. From these cases one might define legal validity: consent is legally valid if and only if it is given voluntarily, the consenting party understands what he is consenting to, and the consent is expressed unambiguously.

But consider a consumer who signs a contract with a large corporation. He reads the terms — all forty pages — and understands them. He signs voluntarily. His consent is unambiguous. The three conditions are met. But the terms are grossly one-sided: the consumer waives virtually all his rights, agrees to binding arbitration in a distant jurisdiction, and accepts unlimited liability for trivial breaches. No reasonable person, one feels, would agree to such terms if they were plainly presented, and yet the consumer did agree, voluntarily and knowingly. The background cases all involved agreements whose substantive terms were fair and reasonable; the definition captured the procedural dimension of consent — voluntariness, understanding, clarity — but dropped the substantive fairness that characterised the motivating examples. It admits agreements that are procedurally valid but substantively unconscionable.

background_cases: A driver who runs a red light and strikes a pedestrian is held liable for the injuries; the driver was at fault.
A surgeon who operates on the wrong limb is held liable for the harm; the surgeon was negligent.
A factory owner whose unsafe machinery injures a worker is held liable; the owner failed to maintain safe conditions.
definition: A person is liable for harm caused to another if and only if (i) the person performed an act or omission, (ii) the act or omission was a cause of the harm, and (iii) the person was at fault, whether through intention, negligence, or recklessness.
conditions: act or omission occurred
act was a cause of harm
person was at fault
counterexample: The owner of a reservoir whose dam, though carefully constructed and maintained, bursts and floods a neighbouring mine is held liable for the damage. The owner was not at fault: every reasonable precaution was taken. Yet the law imposed liability on the principle that one who brings upon one's land something likely to do mischief if it escapes must keep it at one's peril. Fault was entirely absent, yet liability attached.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved clear fault on the part of the person held liable. The definition extracted fault as a necessary condition, but the law also imposes strict liability in cases where the activity is inherently dangerous, regardless of the care exercised by the defendant.
passage:
Consider three cases in which liability follows from fault. A driver who runs a red light and injures a pedestrian is at fault, and liability attaches naturally. A surgeon who operates upon the wrong limb has been negligent, and the law holds the surgeon responsible for the resulting harm. A factory owner whose unsafe machinery injures a worker has failed in the duty to maintain safe conditions, and liability follows. From these cases one might define: a person is liable for harm caused to another if and only if the person performed an act or omission, the act or omission was a cause of the harm, and the person was at fault, whether through intention, negligence, or recklessness.

But consider the celebrated case, drawn from the English reports, of a landowner who constructs a reservoir upon his land. The dam is carefully designed and competently built. Every reasonable precaution is taken. Yet the dam bursts, and the water floods the mines of a neighbouring landowner, causing extensive damage. The reservoir owner was not at fault in any ordinary sense: there was no negligence, no recklessness, no intention to cause harm. Yet the court held the owner liable, on the principle that a person who, for his own purposes, brings upon his land and collects there anything likely to do mischief if it escapes must keep it in at his peril, and is liable for all damage that is the natural consequence of its escape. The background cases all involved fault, and the definition required it. But the law recognises that certain activities are so inherently dangerous that responsibility for the consequences of their going wrong ought to rest on the person who undertook them, regardless of the care exercised. The definition failed to anticipate this category of strict liability.

background_cases: A bystander who witnessed a robbery from close range gives a clear account of the robber's appearance; the testimony is accepted.
A doctor who examined the victim's injuries describes them in detail; the expert testimony is admitted.
A bank clerk who processed the fraudulent cheque identifies the signature as forged; the testimony is received.
definition: Testimony is reliable if and only if (i) the witness perceived the relevant events directly, (ii) the witness possesses the competence to understand and report what was perceived, and (iii) the witness's account is corroborated by other evidence.
conditions: direct perception of events
competence to understand and report
corroborated by other evidence
counterexample: A single eyewitness, who perceived the events directly, is competent, and gives a vivid and detailed account, may be the only witness. There is no corroborating evidence. Yet the testimony may be entirely reliable and may, in some jurisdictions, be sufficient to ground a conviction. The third condition, requiring corroboration, is not met, yet the testimony is reliable.
abductive_insight: The background cases all happened to involve testimony supported by additional evidence. The definition extracted corroboration as a necessary condition, but reliability is a property of the testimony itself — its accuracy and trustworthiness — and a single uncorroborated witness may give perfectly reliable testimony.
passage:
Consider three cases of accepted testimony. A bystander who witnessed a robbery from close range gives a clear account of the robber's appearance and actions; the court accepts the testimony. A doctor who examined the victim's injuries describes them with clinical precision; the expert testimony is admitted. A bank clerk who processed a fraudulent cheque identifies the signature as forged on the basis of professional experience; the testimony is received. In each case, the witness perceived the relevant events directly, possessed the competence to understand and report what was perceived, and the account was corroborated by other evidence — the stolen goods were recovered, the injuries matched the medical report, the forged signature was confirmed by a second expert. One might define: testimony is reliable if and only if the witness perceived the events directly, the witness is competent, and the account is corroborated.

But suppose a woman walking alone at night is the sole witness to a hit-and-run accident. She perceived the events directly and with clarity. She is fully competent to understand and describe what she saw. Her account is detailed, consistent, and given promptly. Yet there is no corroborating evidence: no other witness, no relevant physical evidence, no camera footage. The third condition fails. Yet her testimony may be entirely reliable — accurate in every particular. The background cases all happened to involve corroboration, and the definition extracted this as a necessary condition. But reliability is a property of the testimony itself: its accuracy and its connection to what actually occurred. A single uncorroborated witness may give perfectly reliable testimony, and the law in many jurisdictions recognises that uncorroborated testimony may be sufficient to establish the facts.

background_cases: A statute forbids the sale of 'intoxicating liquors'; a merchant selling whisky is prosecuted. The ordinary meaning of 'intoxicating liquors' plainly includes whisky, and the prosecution succeeds.
A statute requires 'all buildings' to be insured against fire; a warehouse owner who fails to insure is penalised. 'Buildings' plainly includes warehouses.
A statute imposes a tax on 'income from employment'; a salaried clerk is assessed. The ordinary meaning is clear.
definition: A word in a statute bears its ordinary meaning if and only if (i) the word has a settled and commonly understood definition, (ii) that definition produces a sensible result when applied to the facts, and (iii) the context of the statute does not indicate a different meaning.
conditions: word has settled common definition
definition produces sensible result on the facts
context does not indicate different meaning
counterexample: A statute enacted in the eighteenth century grants certain rights to 'all persons.' The word 'persons' has a settled and commonly understood meaning, and the context does not indicate a technical usage. Yet when the statute was enacted, 'persons' was understood to include only men of property. Applied literally to modern conditions, the word would include all human beings. The ordinary meaning has shifted over two centuries, and the question arises whether the word should bear its contemporary or its historical meaning.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved modern statutes applied shortly after enactment, where the ordinary meaning was stable and uncontested. The definition assumed that ordinary meaning is fixed, but language evolves, and a word in an old statute may bear a meaning quite different from its current ordinary sense.
passage:
Consider three cases in which the ordinary meaning of statutory language poses no difficulty. A statute forbids the sale of 'intoxicating liquors,' and a merchant selling whisky is prosecuted; 'intoxicating liquors' plainly includes whisky. A statute requires 'all buildings' to be insured against fire, and a warehouse owner who fails to insure is penalised; 'buildings' plainly includes warehouses. A statute imposes a tax on 'income from employment,' and a salaried clerk is assessed; the ordinary meaning of the words is clear. From these cases one might define: a word in a statute bears its ordinary meaning if and only if the word has a settled and commonly understood definition, that definition produces a sensible result when applied to the facts, and the context of the statute does not indicate a different meaning.

But consider a statute enacted in the eighteenth century that grants certain rights to 'all persons.' The word 'persons' has a settled and commonly understood meaning today, and the context of the statute does not indicate any technical or restricted usage. The three conditions appear to be satisfied, and the word should bear its ordinary meaning. Yet when the statute was enacted, 'persons' was commonly understood in a far narrower sense, often excluding women, servants, and those without property. If one applies the modern ordinary meaning, the statute grants rights to all human beings; if one applies the historical ordinary meaning, the rights are restricted. The definition assumed that ordinary meaning is stable — that the settled definition of a word today is the same as it was when the statute was enacted. The background cases involved modern statutes whose language had not had time to shift. The definition failed to address the problem of linguistic change, which is among the most vexing difficulties in the interpretation of old statutes.

background_cases: A buyer purchases goods from a market stall, pays the asking price, and takes delivery without knowledge of any defect in the seller's title. The buyer acts in good faith.
A lender advances money to a borrower upon production of satisfactory evidence of income and identity. The lender acts in good faith.
A tenant signs a lease after reading its terms and negotiating its provisions. The tenant enters the lease in good faith.
definition: A party acts in good faith if and only if (i) the party acts honestly, (ii) the party does not knowingly take advantage of the other party's ignorance or weakness, and (iii) the party has made reasonable enquiries before acting.
conditions: acts honestly
does not exploit other's ignorance or weakness
has made reasonable enquiries
counterexample: A purchaser of land acts honestly, does not exploit anyone's weakness, and makes reasonable enquiries — consulting the land registry and inspecting the property. Yet the registry contains an error: the land belongs to a third party, not the seller. The purchaser has satisfied all three conditions but has acquired nothing, because the seller had no title to convey. Good faith, though fully present, does not cure the defect in the seller's title.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved transactions in which good faith was both present and sufficient: the buyer got good title, the lender was repaid, the tenant occupied the premises. The definition captured the subjective and procedural requirements of good faith but did not address the question of what good faith achieves. In property law, a bona fide purchaser's good faith may or may not protect against a defect in the chain of title, depending on the legal system.
passage:
Consider three transactions conducted in good faith. A buyer purchases goods from a market stall, pays the asking price, and takes delivery without any knowledge of a defect in the seller's title; the buyer acts in good faith. A lender advances money to a borrower upon satisfactory evidence of income and identity; the lender acts honestly and without exploitation. A tenant signs a lease after reading its terms and negotiating its provisions; the tenant enters the lease in good faith. From these cases one might define: a party acts in good faith if and only if the party acts honestly, does not knowingly exploit the other's ignorance or weakness, and has made reasonable enquiries before acting.

But consider a more troublesome case. A purchaser of land acts honestly, exploits no one, and makes reasonable enquiries — consulting the land registry, inspecting the property, and engaging a solicitor to examine the title deeds. Everything appears to be in order. Yet it transpires that the land registry contains an error: the seller had no title to the land, which in truth belongs to a third party. The purchaser has satisfied all three conditions of the definition and has acted in good faith by any reasonable measure. Yet the purchaser has acquired nothing, for the seller had nothing to convey. The background cases were all transactions in which good faith was not only present but sufficient: the buyer received good title, the lender was repaid, the tenant occupied the premises without disturbance. The definition captured what it means to act in good faith but left unaddressed the question of what good faith achieves. In some legal systems, a bona fide purchaser for value is protected against defects in the chain of title; in others, the true owner's right prevails regardless. Good faith is a necessary condition for many protections, but it is not always a sufficient one.

background_cases: A horse-dealer tells a buyer that a lame horse is sound; the buyer relies on this and purchases the horse. The sale is vitiated by fraud.
A merchant sells adulterated flour as pure, representing it as unadulterated; the buyer, relying on the representation, pays the full price. The transaction is fraudulent.
An insurance applicant falsely states that he has never suffered from a particular illness; the insurer, relying on this, issues the policy. The policy is voidable for fraud.
definition: A transaction is fraudulent if and only if (i) one party made a positive misrepresentation of fact, (ii) the misrepresentation was made with knowledge of its falsity or with reckless disregard of its truth, and (iii) the other party was induced by the misrepresentation to enter the transaction.
conditions: positive misrepresentation of fact
made knowingly or recklessly
induced the other party to transact
counterexample: A seller of a house knows that the cellar is prone to severe flooding but, when asked about the condition of the property, says only 'The house is in good order,' omitting any mention of the flooding. The statement is literally true as to the structure, and the seller has made no positively false claim about the cellar. Yet the deliberate suppression of a material fact — the flooding — in response to a direct enquiry has the same practical effect as a lie. The first condition, requiring a positive misrepresentation, is not clearly met.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved affirmative lies — statements that were positively and unambiguously false. The definition extracted the requirement of a positive misrepresentation from these clear cases, but fraud may equally consist in the deliberate suppression or concealment of a material fact, particularly where there is a duty of disclosure or where a half-truth creates a misleading impression.
passage:
Consider three transactions vitiated by fraud. A horse-dealer tells a buyer that a lame horse is sound; the buyer relies on this and purchases the horse at a price appropriate only for a sound animal. A merchant sells adulterated flour as pure, making an explicit representation of quality that is false. An insurance applicant falsely declares himself free of a particular illness, and the insurer, relying on this representation, issues a policy. In each case one party made a positive misrepresentation, made it knowingly, and thereby induced the other party to enter a transaction. One might define: a transaction is fraudulent if and only if one party made a positive misrepresentation of fact, the misrepresentation was made with knowledge of its falsity or with reckless disregard of its truth, and the other party was induced by it to enter the transaction.

But consider a seller of a house who knows that the cellar is subject to severe flooding after every heavy rain. When the buyer asks about the condition of the property, the seller says, 'The house is in good order,' which is, as to the visible structure, literally true. The seller says nothing at all about the cellar or the flooding. No positively false statement has been made: the seller has not said, 'The cellar is dry.' Yet the deliberate suppression of a material fact, in response to a direct enquiry about the property's condition, creates a misleading impression as damaging as any explicit lie. The background cases all involved affirmative falsehoods — clear, positive statements contradicted by the facts. The definition was drawn from these and required a positive misrepresentation. But fraud may equally consist in the calculated concealment of what one is under a duty to disclose, or in the utterance of a half-truth that, while technically accurate, is designed to mislead. The definition, by requiring a positive misrepresentation, has failed to encompass the subtler but no less culpable species of fraud by silence and suppression.

background_cases: A court of equity grants specific performance of a contract for the sale of a rare painting, because damages would not adequately compensate the buyer for the loss of a unique object.
A court of equity grants an injunction to restrain a nuisance — a factory emitting noxious fumes — because damages would not remedy the ongoing harm to the plaintiff's health.
definition: A court may intervene in equity if and only if (i) the case involves a unique or irreplaceable subject matter, (ii) the plaintiff has no adequate remedy at law, and (iii) the equitable remedy sought is specific performance or injunction.
conditions: case involves unique or irreplaceable subject matter
plaintiff has no adequate remedy at law
remedy sought is specific performance or injunction
counterexample: A court of equity may rescind a contract that was procured by undue influence — for instance, a gift by an elderly person to her carer, obtained through psychological pressure. The subject matter (money) is not unique or irreplaceable. The remedy is rescission, not specific performance or injunction. The first and third conditions fail, yet the court properly exercises its equitable jurisdiction.
abductive_insight: The background cases both involved unique assets and prohibitory or compulsory remedies. The definition treated uniqueness of subject matter and the specific remedies of performance and injunction as essential, but equity also encompasses rescission, rectification, and the undoing of transactions tainted by unconscionable conduct, regardless of whether the subject matter is unique.
passage:
Consider two equitable interventions. A court grants specific performance of a contract for a rare painting, because no sum of money can substitute for a unique artwork. A court grants an injunction to restrain a factory from emitting noxious fumes, because damages cannot remedy ongoing harm to the plaintiff's health. From these cases one might define equity: a court may intervene in equity if and only if the case involves a unique or irreplaceable subject matter, the plaintiff has no adequate remedy at law, and the equitable remedy sought is specific performance or injunction.

But this definition, drawn from cases of unique assets and prohibitory remedies, is too narrow. Consider the equitable remedy of rescission. An elderly woman, enfeebled by age and dependent upon her carer, makes a large gift to the carer under psychological pressure. The gift is voidable for undue influence, and the court rescinds the transaction, restoring the money to the elderly woman. The subject matter — money — is not unique or irreplaceable; money is fungible. The remedy is not specific performance or injunction but rescission — the undoing of the transaction. The first and third conditions fail. Yet the court is properly exercising its equitable jurisdiction, intervening to set aside a transaction that was procured by unconscionable conduct. The background cases both involved unique assets and the remedies of performance and injunction. The definition mistook these features for the boundaries of equity, overlooking the vast body of equitable doctrine — undue influence, unconscionability, constructive trust, rectification — that operates without regard to the uniqueness of the subject matter and employs remedies quite different from specific performance and injunction.

background_cases: A landlord represents to his tenant that a boundary wall belongs to the landlord's property; the tenant relies on this and does not repair the wall. The landlord is later estopped from claiming that the wall belongs to the tenant.
A merchant represents to a buyer that goods are of a certain quality; the buyer relies on the representation and purchases the goods. The merchant is estopped from denying the quality he represented.
definition: A party is estopped if and only if (i) he made a voluntary representation, (ii) the representation concerned a present fact, and (iii) the other party suffered quantifiable financial loss by relying upon it.
conditions: made voluntary representation
representation concerned present fact
other party suffered quantifiable financial loss
counterexample: Under proprietary estoppel, a landowner who informally assures his neighbour that 'the cottage will be yours when I die,' leading the neighbour to spend years improving the property in reliance upon the assurance, may be estopped from denying the neighbour's interest. The representation concerns a future intention, not a present fact. And the neighbour's loss may not be easily quantifiable in financial terms — it includes years of labour and the reordering of his life. The second and third conditions fail, yet the estoppel operates.
abductive_insight: The background cases both involved representations of present fact that caused measurable financial loss. The definition abstracted these features as essential, but proprietary estoppel shows that the doctrine may operate upon representations of future intention, and the detriment suffered may be practical and personal rather than strictly quantifiable in monetary terms.
passage:
Consider two cases of estoppel. A landlord tells his tenant that a boundary wall belongs to the landlord; the tenant relies on this and does not repair it. The landlord is later estopped from claiming otherwise. A merchant tells a buyer that goods are of a certain quality; the buyer purchases in reliance. The merchant is estopped from denying the quality. From these cases one might define estoppel: a party is estopped if and only if he made a voluntary representation, the representation concerned a present fact, and the other party suffered quantifiable financial loss by relying upon it.

But this definition, drawn from commercial representations of fact, is too narrow. Consider proprietary estoppel. An elderly landowner tells his neighbour, informally but sincerely, that 'the cottage will be yours when I die.' The neighbour, relying upon this assurance, spends years improving the property — repairing the roof, cultivating the garden, maintaining the walls — in the expectation that the cottage will one day be his. The landowner dies, and his heirs deny the neighbour's claim. The court holds that the heirs are estopped from denying the neighbour's interest, because the landowner's assurance created an expectation upon which the neighbour relied to his detriment. But the representation concerned a future intention — 'will be yours when I die' — not a present fact. And the neighbour's detriment is not easily quantifiable in financial terms; it includes years of labour, the restructuring of his plans, and the emotional investment of a life arranged around the assurance. The second and third conditions fail. The background cases involved factual representations causing measurable loss. The definition mistook these for essential features, but proprietary estoppel extends the doctrine to promises about the future and to detriment that is practical and personal rather than narrowly financial.

background_cases: A bank accidentally credits ten thousand pounds to the wrong customer's account; the customer is enriched by the bank's mistake, and the bank claims restitution.
A man pays a debt to the wrong creditor owing to a confusion of names; the recipient is unjustly enriched, and the payer claims restitution.
definition: A claim in restitution lies if and only if (i) the claimant transferred a determinate sum of money to the defendant, (ii) the transfer was vitiated by a mistake of fact, and (iii) the defendant was a passive recipient who did nothing to procure the enrichment.
conditions: claimant transferred determinate sum of money
transfer vitiated by mistake of fact
defendant passive recipient
counterexample: A builder who, acting under a contract that is later declared void, constructs an extension on the defendant's house has a claim in restitution for the value of the work done. The enrichment is not a transfer of money but the conferral of a service; the vitiating factor is not a mistake of fact but the invalidity of the contract; and the defendant was not a passive recipient — he actively requested the work. All three conditions fail, yet restitution is available.
abductive_insight: The background cases both involved mistaken payments of money to passive recipients. The definition abstracted monetary transfer, factual mistake, and passivity as essential, but restitution extends to the conferral of non-monetary benefits, to enrichment arising from void contracts and other vitiating factors, and to defendants who actively sought the benefit.
passage:
Consider two cases of restitution. A bank accidentally credits ten thousand pounds to the wrong customer; the bank claims restitution. A man pays a debt to the wrong creditor owing to a confusion of names; the payer claims restitution. In each case, a determinate sum of money was transferred, the transfer was caused by a mistake of fact, and the recipient was passive — he did nothing to procure the windfall. From these cases one might define restitution: a claim lies if and only if the claimant transferred a determinate sum of money, the transfer was vitiated by a mistake of fact, and the defendant was a passive recipient.

But this definition, drawn from mistaken-payment cases, is too narrow. Consider a builder who constructs an extension on the defendant's house under a contract that is later declared void — perhaps because one of the parties lacked capacity, or because a statutory requirement was not observed. The defendant's property has been improved; he is enriched. The builder seeks restitution for the value of the work done. But the enrichment is not a transfer of money; it is the conferral of a building service. The vitiating factor is not a mistake of fact but the invalidity of the contract. And the defendant was not a passive recipient; he actively engaged the builder and supervised the work. All three conditions fail. Yet the builder has a legitimate claim in restitution, because the defendant has been enriched at the builder's expense and it would be unjust for him to retain the benefit of the work without paying for it. The background cases were monetary transfers to passive recipients. The definition mistook these features for essential elements, overlooking the many situations in which restitution operates to reverse non-monetary enrichment, vitiated by grounds other than factual mistake, and against defendants who actively participated in the transaction.

background_cases: A county court in England exercises jurisdiction over a breach-of-contract dispute between two local residents: the parties live within the court's district, the cause of action arose there, and the court is empowered by statute to hear such claims.
A Crown Court exercises jurisdiction over a criminal case: the offence was committed within its circuit, the defendant was arrested there, and the court has criminal jurisdiction by statute.
definition: A court has jurisdiction if and only if (i) the relevant events occurred within the court's territorial district, (ii) both parties are domiciled within that district, and (iii) the subject matter falls within the court's statutory competence.
conditions: relevant events occurred within territorial district
both parties domiciled within district
subject matter within statutory competence
counterexample: An English court may exercise jurisdiction over a dispute arising from a contract performed in France between a Frenchman and a German, if the contract contains a clause submitting disputes to the jurisdiction of the English courts. Neither party is domiciled in England, and the relevant events occurred abroad. The first and second conditions fail, yet the court has jurisdiction by virtue of the parties' agreement.
abductive_insight: The background cases both involved purely local disputes in which territory, domicile, and subject matter were aligned. The definition treated territorial connexion and domicile as essential, but party autonomy — the freedom to choose a forum by agreement — is a well-established basis of jurisdiction that operates independently of where the events occurred or where the parties reside.
passage:
Consider two cases of jurisdiction. A county court hears a breach-of-contract dispute between two local residents: both live within its district, the contract was performed there, and the court has statutory authority over such claims. A Crown Court tries a criminal case: the offence was committed within its circuit, the defendant was arrested there, and the court has criminal jurisdiction. From these cases one might define jurisdiction: a court has jurisdiction if and only if the relevant events occurred within the court's territorial district, both parties are domiciled there, and the subject matter falls within the court's statutory competence.

But this definition, drawn from purely local disputes, proves too restrictive. Consider a commercial contract between a French exporter and a German importer for the sale of goods to be delivered in Hamburg. The contract contains a clause providing that any disputes arising under the contract shall be submitted to the exclusive jurisdiction of the English High Court. A dispute arises, and the French exporter commences proceedings in London. Neither party is domiciled in England. The relevant events — the negotiation, the performance, the breach — all occurred on the Continent. The first and second conditions fail entirely. Yet the English court has jurisdiction, because the parties freely agreed to submit their disputes to it, and the law respects their autonomy in the choice of forum. The background cases were local disputes in which territory, domicile, and subject matter were conveniently aligned. The definition mistook this alignment for a universal requirement, overlooking the principle of party autonomy, which permits parties to confer jurisdiction on a court that has no other connexion with the dispute.

background_cases: A criminal trial in which the defendant is informed of the charges, represented by counsel, given the opportunity to cross-examine witnesses, and judged by an impartial jury.
An administrative hearing in which a government employee facing dismissal is notified in writing, afforded a hearing before a panel, and permitted to present evidence in his defence.
definition: A proceeding satisfies due process if and only if (i) the affected person is represented by legal counsel, (ii) the proceeding follows a fixed, predetermined procedure, and (iii) the decision-maker provides written reasons for the decision.
conditions: affected person represented by legal counsel
proceeding follows fixed predetermined procedure
decision-maker provides written reasons
counterexample: The disciplinary proceedings of a school satisfy due process in a meaningful sense: the pupil is told what he is accused of, given an opportunity to explain himself, and judged by a person who has no personal interest in the outcome. Yet the pupil is not represented by counsel, the procedure is not fixed in the manner of a court (the headmaster may adapt it to the circumstances), and the headmaster may communicate his decision orally rather than in writing. All three conditions fail, yet the proceeding is fair and satisfies a recognisable standard of due process.
abductive_insight: The background cases were both formal legal proceedings with professional representation, codified procedures, and written decisions. The definition abstracted these features as essential, but the school disciplinary hearing shows that due process is a flexible concept whose core — notice, an opportunity to be heard, and impartial decision-making — may be satisfied without formal representation, rigid procedure, or written reasons.
passage:
Consider two proceedings. A criminal trial: the defendant is informed of the charges, represented by counsel, permitted to cross-examine witnesses, and tried by an impartial jury. An administrative hearing: the employee is notified in writing, given a hearing before a panel, and allowed to present evidence. From these cases one might define due process: a proceeding satisfies due process if and only if the affected person is represented by legal counsel, the proceeding follows a fixed, predetermined procedure, and the decision-maker provides written reasons.

But this definition, drawn from formal legal proceedings, is too restrictive. Consider the disciplinary proceedings of a school. A pupil is accused of misconduct — cheating in an examination, say. The headmaster summons him, informs him of the accusation, and invites him to give his account. The headmaster listens, considers the evidence, and decides whether to impose a punishment. The pupil is not represented by counsel; his parents may be present, but no solicitor attends. The procedure is not fixed in the manner of a court; the headmaster adapts it to the gravity of the offence and the age of the pupil. And the decision may be communicated orally rather than in writing. All three conditions fail. Yet the proceeding is fair. The pupil has been told what he is accused of, given a chance to respond, and judged by a person with no personal interest in the outcome. The core requirements of due process — notice, an opportunity to be heard, and impartiality — are satisfied. The background cases were formal legal proceedings, and the definition mistook their formal apparatus for the essence of due process. But due process is a flexible standard, and its fundamental requirements may be met in settings far less formal than the courtroom or the administrative tribunal.

background_cases: A man leaves his overcoat at a restaurant cloakroom; the cloakroom attendant accepts it and undertakes to return it when the man departs.
A jeweller receives a ring for repair; he takes possession of the ring and undertakes to return it upon completion of the work.
definition: A bailment exists if and only if (i) the bailee has actual physical custody of the chattel, (ii) the bailee has consented to receive it, and (iii) both parties intend that the chattel shall be returned.
conditions: bailee has actual physical custody
bailee consented to receive chattel
both parties intend chattel to be returned
counterexample: A man parks his motorcar in an enclosed car park, locks it, and retains the key. The car park operator has constructive but not actual physical custody of the vehicle — the operator cannot move the car or open it. The operator did not consent to receive the specific vehicle; he merely made the space available. Yet a bailment exists, and the operator owes duties of care in respect of the car. The first and second conditions fail in their strict senses, yet the bailment is recognised.
abductive_insight: The background cases both involved the physical handover of a portable object to a person who expressly accepted it. The definition abstracted physical custody and express consent as essential, but the car park case shows that constructive possession — control over the space in which the goods are situated — may suffice, and consent may be implied from the nature of the transaction rather than expressly given.
passage:
Consider two bailments. A man leaves his overcoat at a cloakroom; the attendant takes it, hangs it up, and will return it when called for. A jeweller receives a ring for repair; he takes the ring into his workshop and will return it when the work is done. In each case the bailee has actual physical custody, he has consented to receive the chattel, and both parties intend that it shall be returned. From these cases one might define bailment: a bailment exists if and only if the bailee has actual physical custody, the bailee has consented to receive the chattel, and both parties intend the chattel to be returned.

But this definition, drawn from the hand-to-hand delivery of portable objects, is too narrow. Consider a man who parks his motorcar in an enclosed car park, locks the doors, and retains the key. The car park operator cannot open the car, move it, or exercise physical control over it in the way that the cloakroom attendant controls the overcoat. His possession is constructive rather than actual — he controls the premises in which the car is situated, but not the car itself. Nor did he consent to receive this particular vehicle; he merely made a parking space available on general terms. Yet the law recognises a bailment: the car park operator owes duties of care, and if the car is stolen or damaged through his negligence, he is liable. The first and second conditions fail in their strict senses. The background cases involved physical handover and express acceptance. The definition mistook these features for essential elements, but constructive possession — control over the space in which goods are kept — may suffice, and consent may be implied from the nature of the commercial arrangement rather than expressly given for each chattel.

background_cases: A merchant offers to sell goods to a buyer at a fixed price; the buyer accepts in writing; both parties perform their obligations. The agreement is enforceable.
A landlord offers to lease premises to a tenant for a stated rent; the tenant accepts in writing; both parties perform. The lease is enforceable.
A publisher offers to pay an author a royalty for the right to publish her book; the author accepts in writing; both parties perform. The agreement is enforceable.
definition: An agreement is a binding contract if and only if (i) one party makes an offer specifying definite terms, (ii) the other party accepts the offer in writing, and (iii) both parties intend to create legal relations.
conditions: one party makes an offer specifying definite terms
the other party accepts the offer in writing
both parties intend to create legal relations
counterexample: A farmer telephones a neighbouring farmer and offers to sell him a horse for fifty pounds. The neighbour says 'I accept' over the telephone and collects the horse the following morning, paying the agreed sum. Both parties intended the transaction to be binding. The offer specified definite terms and acceptance was given, yet the acceptance was oral, not written. The agreement is nonetheless enforceable, for the law does not require acceptance to be in writing for a contract of this kind.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved written acceptances — a letter, a signed lease, a publishing agreement — and the definition abstracted writing as a necessary mode of acceptance. But writing was merely a feature common to the particular cases examined; it is not a requirement of the law of contract in general. Acceptance may be communicated by any means sufficient to bring it to the offeror's attention, including oral communication, conduct, or even silence in certain circumstances. The definition mistook a contingent feature of the sample for an essential element of the concept.
passage:
Consider three agreements. A merchant offers goods at a fixed price and the buyer accepts in writing; a landlord offers a lease and the tenant accepts in writing; a publisher offers royalties and the author accepts in writing. Each is enforceable, and each involves a written acceptance. One might extract from these cases a definition: an agreement is a binding contract if and only if one party makes an offer specifying definite terms, the other party accepts the offer in writing, and both parties intend to create legal relations. The background cases all exhibit written acceptances, and the definition elevates this feature to a necessary condition.

But the definition is too restrictive. Consider a farmer who telephones his neighbour and offers to sell a horse for fifty pounds. The neighbour, without hesitation, says over the telephone that he accepts the offer. The following morning the neighbour arrives with the money, pays, and leads the horse away. Both parties understood the transaction to be binding; the offer specified definite terms; acceptance was clearly communicated. Yet no writing was involved at any stage. The acceptance was oral, conveyed by telephone, and the contract was performed without difficulty. The law does not require that acceptance be in writing for contracts of this kind. The Statute of Frauds imposes a writing requirement for certain classes of contract — contracts for the sale of land, guarantees, and certain others — but for the ordinary sale of goods or chattels, oral acceptance is perfectly sufficient. The background cases happened to involve written acceptances because they concerned transactions of a kind — commercial sales, leases, publishing agreements — in which the parties commonly reduce their agreements to writing for reasons of convenience and clarity, not because the law demands it. The definition mistook a practice that is prudent for a condition that is necessary, and in so doing it excluded a vast number of binding contracts formed by oral acceptance or by conduct.

background_cases: A landowner grants a right of way across his field to a neighbour by executing a written deed. The easement is valid and enforceable.
A developer grants an easement of drainage to an adjoining landowner by means of a formal conveyance. The easement is valid.
A farmer grants a right to draw water from a spring on his land to a neighbouring farm, the grant being made by sealed instrument. The easement is valid.
definition: An easement over land is created if and only if (i) the owner of the servient tenement executes a written grant, (ii) the grant identifies the dominant and servient tenements with reasonable particularity, and (iii) the grant is supported by consideration.
conditions: the owner of the servient tenement executes a written grant
the grant identifies the dominant and servient tenements with reasonable particularity
the grant is supported by consideration
counterexample: A landowner sells a portion of his estate. The portion sold is entirely enclosed by the land retained by the vendor and has no access to any public road. No express grant of a right of way is included in the conveyance, and no consideration is given for such a right. Yet the law implies an easement of necessity in favour of the purchaser, for without it the land sold would be wholly unusable. The easement arises by operation of law, not by written grant, and requires no separate consideration.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all express grants made by written instrument and supported by consideration. The definition generalised these features as necessary conditions. But easements may arise not only by express grant but also by implication and by necessity, where the circumstances of a conveyance demand it. The cases examined were all instances of one mode of creation — the express grant — and the definition failed to recognise that the law provides alternative modes for cases in which an express grant was never made but the existence of the easement is logically entailed by the transaction.
passage:
Consider three grants of easements. A landowner grants a right of way by deed; a developer grants an easement of drainage by formal conveyance; a farmer grants a right to draw water by sealed instrument. In each case the easement is created by an express written grant supported by consideration. One might conclude that an easement over land is created if and only if the owner of the servient tenement executes a written grant, the grant identifies the dominant and servient tenements with reasonable particularity, and the grant is supported by consideration. The definition captures neatly the mechanism common to all three cases.

Yet the definition is incomplete. Suppose a landowner sells a portion of his estate to a purchaser. The portion sold is entirely enclosed by land that the vendor retains; it has no frontage upon any public road and no other means of access. The conveyance makes no mention of any right of way, and no separate consideration is given for one. Taken literally, the proposed definition would deny the existence of an easement, since there is no written grant and no consideration. But the result would be absurd: the purchaser would own a parcel of land to which he could not lawfully gain access, and the land would be rendered wholly useless. The law avoids this absurdity through the doctrine of easement by necessity, which implies a right of way in favour of a grantee whose land would otherwise be landlocked. The easement arises by operation of law from the circumstances of the conveyance, without any express grant and without any separate consideration. The background cases were all instances of express grants — the most formal and deliberate mode of creating an easement — and the definition extrapolated from these cases to a general rule. But the law recognises that not all easements originate in express agreements. Some arise from the logic of the situation, implied by the law because to deny them would defeat the very purpose of the transaction from which they arise.

background_cases: A physician who fails to diagnose a common illness, causing the patient's condition to worsen, is found negligent. The physician owed and breached a duty of care to the patient.
A solicitor who misses a limitation period, causing the client to lose a viable claim, is found negligent. The solicitor owed and breached a duty of care to the client.
An architect who designs a structurally unsound building, causing it to collapse, is found negligent. The architect owed and breached a duty of care to the client.
definition: A professional person is negligent if and only if (i) the professional owes a duty of care to the claimant arising from a direct professional relationship, (ii) the professional's conduct falls below the standard expected of a competent practitioner in that field, and (iii) the breach causes the claimant quantifiable financial loss.
conditions: the professional owes a duty of care arising from a direct professional relationship
the professional's conduct falls below the standard expected of a competent practitioner
the breach causes the claimant quantifiable financial loss
counterexample: A solicitor is instructed to draft a will leaving a bequest to a named beneficiary. The solicitor negligently fails to ensure that the will is properly witnessed, and the will is declared invalid after the testator's death. The intended beneficiary — who had no professional relationship with the solicitor — suffers the loss of the bequest. The first condition fails, yet the solicitor may be held liable to the disappointed beneficiary, for the law has recognised that in certain circumstances the duty of care extends to persons who are not the professional's own client but who are foreseeably affected by the professional's negligence.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved claims by direct clients — patients, clients, building owners — and the definition required a direct professional relationship as a condition of the duty of care. But this was merely a feature of the particular cases sampled. The law has extended the professional's duty of care beyond the immediate client to third parties who are foreseeably and proximately affected by the professional's negligence, particularly where the very purpose of the professional engagement is to confer a benefit upon the third party, as in the drafting of a will for the benefit of a named legatee.
passage:
Consider three cases of professional negligence. A physician fails to diagnose a common illness, and the patient suffers; a solicitor misses a limitation period, and the client loses a claim; an architect designs an unsound structure, and the building collapses. In each case the professional owed a duty of care to the claimant arising from a direct professional relationship, the professional's conduct fell below the standard expected of a competent practitioner, and the breach caused the claimant quantifiable financial loss. One might generalise: a professional person is negligent if and only if these three conditions are satisfied. The definition seems to follow naturally from the cases.

But consider the following situation. A solicitor is instructed by an elderly client to prepare a will leaving a legacy of five thousand pounds to a named beneficiary — let us say, a niece. The solicitor drafts the will but negligently fails to arrange for it to be properly witnessed before the testator's death. The testator dies, the will is declared invalid, and the niece loses the legacy. The niece was not the solicitor's client; she had no professional relationship with the solicitor whatever. She may not even have known that the will was being drafted. Yet the solicitor's negligence has caused her a clear and quantifiable loss — the value of the bequest she would have received had the will been valid. The first condition of the proposed definition fails: there is no direct professional relationship between the solicitor and the niece. Yet the law has held, in cases closely resembling this one, that the solicitor owes a duty of care to the intended beneficiary. The rationale is that the very purpose of the solicitor's engagement was to confer a benefit upon the beneficiary, and if the solicitor's negligence defeats that purpose, the beneficiary is the person who suffers the loss. The background cases all involved direct clients, and the definition abstracted the direct relationship as essential. But the law is more capacious: where a third party is so closely and foreseeably affected by the professional's work that justice requires the extension of the duty, the absence of a direct professional relationship is not a bar.

background_cases: A man of thirty-five years enters into a contract for the purchase of a house. He is of sound mind and full age. The contract is valid and enforceable against him.
A woman of fifty years executes a deed of gift in favour of her grandchild. She is of sound mind and full age. The deed is valid.
A man of twenty-eight years makes a will disposing of his estate. He is of sound mind and full age. The will is valid.
definition: A natural person possesses full legal capacity if and only if (i) the person has attained the age of majority, (ii) the person is of sound mind, and (iii) the person is acting voluntarily and without external compulsion.
conditions: the person has attained the age of majority
the person is of sound mind
the person is acting voluntarily and without external compulsion
counterexample: A minor of seventeen years, living independently and earning his own livelihood, enters into a contract for the purchase of necessaries — food, clothing, and lodging. He is below the age of majority, so the first condition fails. Yet the contract is enforceable against him, for the law has long recognised that minors possess the capacity to contract for necessaries, even though they lack full contractual capacity in general.
abductive_insight: The background cases all featured adults of full age, and the definition treated attainment of the age of majority as a necessary condition of legal capacity. But the cases were drawn from a population of adult actors, and the definition overgeneralised from this sample. The law recognises partial capacity in minors, permitting them to enter into binding contracts for necessaries — goods and services suited to their condition in life and actually required by them. The age of majority is a sufficient condition for full capacity, but it is not a necessary condition for all capacity, and the definition's failure to distinguish between full and partial capacity led it astray.
passage:
Consider three acts. A man of thirty-five purchases a house; a woman of fifty executes a deed of gift; a man of twenty-eight makes a will. Each is of full age and sound mind, each acts voluntarily, and each act is legally valid. From these cases one might extract a definition: a natural person possesses full legal capacity if and only if the person has attained the age of majority, the person is of sound mind, and the person is acting voluntarily and without external compulsion.

But the definition, though it captures the standard case, is too sweeping in its exclusions. Consider a youth of seventeen years who has left the parental home, established himself in lodgings, and taken up regular employment. He enters into a contract with a tailor for the supply of clothes suitable to his station, and with a grocer for the supply of food. He is below the age of majority; the first condition fails. Yet these contracts are binding upon him. The common law has long distinguished between contracts for necessaries and contracts of other kinds, and it has held that minors — persons below the age of majority — possess the capacity to contract for necessaries, that is to say, for goods and services suited to their condition in life and actually needed by them. The rationale is both humane and practical: if a minor could not bind himself for necessaries, no tradesman would supply him, and he would be left without the means of subsistence. The background cases were all drawn from the experience of adults, and the definition generalised the feature of majority as though it were indispensable to any exercise of legal capacity. But the law is more refined. It recognises degrees of capacity, permitting minors to act in certain spheres whilst withholding full capacity until the age of majority is reached. The definition's error was to treat a sufficient condition for full capacity as a necessary condition for any capacity whatever.

background_cases: A patient signs a consent form before surgery, having been informed of the risks and alternatives by her surgeon. The consent is valid.
A research subject signs a consent form before participating in a clinical trial, having been informed of the procedures and risks. The consent is valid.
A donor signs a consent form before giving blood, having been advised of the process and any discomfort involved. The consent is valid.
definition: A person's consent to a medical intervention is valid if and only if (i) the person signs a written consent form, (ii) the person has been informed of the material risks and alternatives, and (iii) the person has had an opportunity to ask questions.
conditions: the person signs a written consent form
the person has been informed of the material risks and alternatives
the person has had an opportunity to ask questions
counterexample: An unconscious man is brought to hospital following a road accident. He is in immediate danger of death and requires emergency surgery. He cannot sign a consent form, cannot receive information about risks, and cannot ask questions. All three conditions fail. Yet the surgeon proceeds lawfully, for the law recognises that in circumstances of emergency, where the patient is incapable of giving consent and delay would be life-threatening, treatment may be administered without consent under the doctrine of necessity.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved conscious, competent patients who gave their consent through a deliberate process of information, reflection, and signature. The definition generalised these features as necessary conditions. But all the cases were drawn from elective or planned procedures where the patient was capable of participating in the consent process. The definition failed to consider the emergency case, in which the patient is unconscious and cannot participate at all. The law accommodates this situation through the doctrine of necessity, permitting treatment without consent where it is immediately required to preserve life. The definition mistook the features of non-urgent care for universal requirements of valid medical intervention.
passage:
Consider three cases of medical consent. A patient signs a form before surgery, having been informed of the risks; a research subject signs before a clinical trial, having been advised of the procedures; a blood donor signs before donation, having been told of the process. Each consent is valid. From these cases one might define valid consent to medical intervention: a person's consent is valid if and only if the person signs a written consent form, the person has been informed of the material risks and alternatives, and the person has had an opportunity to ask questions.

Now consider a man who is involved in a serious road accident and is brought to hospital unconscious. He is haemorrhaging internally and will die within the hour unless emergency surgery is performed. He cannot sign a consent form because he is unconscious. He cannot be informed of risks because he cannot hear or comprehend. He cannot ask questions because he cannot speak. Every condition of the proposed definition fails. If the definition is correct, the surgeon cannot lawfully operate, and the man will die. But this is not the law. The doctrine of necessity permits medical practitioners to administer treatment without consent where the patient is incapable of consenting and the treatment is immediately necessary to preserve life or prevent serious deterioration. The surgeon who operates in such circumstances acts lawfully and indeed is under a professional duty to do so. The background cases were all instances of elective or planned procedures involving conscious and competent patients, and the definition extracted from them a set of conditions appropriate to that context alone. It failed to account for the emergency case, in which none of the ordinary conditions of consent can be fulfilled, yet in which the law permits — and morality demands — that treatment proceed. The definition confused the procedural safeguards appropriate to planned care with the universal conditions of lawful treatment, and in so doing it rendered unlawful the very interventions that the law most urgently endorses.

background_cases: A man is attacked in the street by an assailant wielding a knife. He strikes the assailant with his fist, disabling him. The force is proportionate and his action is lawful self-defence.

A woman is set upon by a mugger who seizes her handbag and threatens her with a broken bottle. She pushes the mugger to the ground and retrieves her property. The force is proportionate and her action is lawful self-defence.

A shopkeeper is confronted by a robber who levels a firearm at him. The shopkeeper strikes the robber with a heavy object, incapacitating him. The force is proportionate and his action is lawful self-defence.
definition: A person acts in lawful self-defence if and only if (i) the person is attacked or immediately threatened with unlawful violence, (ii) the person uses force that is proportionate to the attack, and (iii) the person uses no more force than is necessary to repel the attack and then desists.
conditions: the person is attacked or immediately threatened with unlawful violence
the person uses force that is proportionate to the attack
the person uses no more force than is necessary to repel the attack and then desists
counterexample: A householder returns home to find a burglar in his living room, rifling through his possessions. The burglar is not engaged in violence and has made no threat; upon seeing the householder he attempts to flee. The householder, acting in the heat of the moment and in genuine fear, strikes the burglar to prevent his escape. The householder was not attacked or immediately threatened with violence — the burglar was trying to flee — and the force used was arguably more than necessary to repel an attack that was not occurring. Yet the law in a number of jurisdictions provides that a householder who uses force against an intruder in his dwelling is to be judged by the standard of what was honest and instinctive rather than by a detached assessment of proportionality, recognising that persons confronted with intruders in their homes cannot be expected to weigh the niceties of the law.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved clear, ongoing attacks by armed or violent assailants, and the definition abstracted from them a requirement of proportionate response to an imminent attack. But these cases represented the paradigmatic scenario of self-defence in a public place against an actively violent aggressor. They did not include the case of the householder who confronts an intruder in his own dwelling in circumstances of surprise and fear. The law has developed special rules for this situation, permitting a more generous assessment of the force used by householders, and the definition's strict requirement of proportionate response to an ongoing attack cannot accommodate these rules.
passage:
Consider three cases of self-defence. A man is attacked in the street with a knife and disables his assailant with a blow; a woman repels a mugger who threatens her with a broken bottle; a shopkeeper incapacitates a robber who points a firearm at him. In each case the defender was attacked or immediately threatened with unlawful violence, the force used was proportionate, and the defender desisted once the threat was neutralised. One might define lawful self-defence accordingly: a person acts in lawful self-defence if and only if the person is attacked or immediately threatened with unlawful violence, the person uses force proportionate to the attack, and the person desists once the threat is repelled.

But consider a householder who returns home in the evening to find a burglar in his living room. The burglar has forced entry, has scattered the householder's possessions about the floor, and is in the act of gathering valuables. Upon seeing the householder, the burglar drops the items and moves towards the window to escape. The householder, startled and frightened, strikes the burglar before the burglar can climb out. The householder was not, strictly speaking, attacked or immediately threatened with violence — the burglar was attempting flight, not assault. The force used was not proportionate to any ongoing attack, because no attack was occurring. Yet the law in several jurisdictions has recognised that a person who encounters an intruder in his own home is placed in a situation of acute alarm and cannot be expected to make a calm and measured assessment of the precise degree of force that is justified. The standard applied is not one of strict proportionality but of honest and instinctive reaction. The background cases all involved confrontations in public places with clearly violent assailants, and the definition abstracted conditions appropriate to those circumstances. It did not account for the special position of the householder, whose response is judged by a more generous standard because of the unique vulnerability and fear attendant upon finding an intruder in one's own dwelling.

background_cases: A motorist runs a red light and strikes a cyclist, breaking the cyclist's arm. But for the motorist's act, the cyclist would not have been injured. The motorist's negligence is the cause of the injury.
A factory owner discharges toxic waste into a stream, killing the fish. But for the discharge, the fish would have survived. The factory owner's act is the cause of the loss.

A builder uses substandard materials in the construction of a wall, and the wall collapses, damaging a neighbouring property. But for the use of substandard materials, the wall would not have collapsed. The builder's negligence is the cause of the damage.
definition: An act is the cause of an injury if and only if (i) the injury would not have occurred but for the act, (ii) the act was a necessary condition of the injury, and (iii) no other sufficient cause of the injury existed.
conditions: the injury would not have occurred but for the act
the act was a necessary condition of the injury
no other sufficient cause of the injury existed
counterexample: Two hunters, acting independently and without concert, simultaneously fire their shotguns negligently in the direction of a bystander. Each discharge alone would have been sufficient to cause the injury the bystander suffers. The but-for test fails for each hunter individually: the injury would have occurred even if one of them had not fired, because the other's shot was independently sufficient. The first and second conditions fail for each defendant, and another sufficient cause exists, so the third condition fails as well. Yet both hunters are held liable as concurrent tortfeasors, for the law refuses to permit each wrongdoer to escape by pointing to the sufficiency of the other's act.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved single causes operating in isolation — one motorist, one factory owner, one builder — and the but-for test worked perfectly in each case because removing the defendant's act would have prevented the injury. The definition generalised this to require that the act be a necessary condition and that no other sufficient cause exist. But these conditions were artefacts of the simplicity of the sample cases. In cases of overdetermination, where two independently sufficient causes operate simultaneously, the but-for test breaks down because neither act is a necessary condition of the injury. The law addresses such cases by abandoning the but-for test in favour of a test of material contribution or substantial factor, holding each concurrent tortfeasor liable despite the logical failure of the necessity condition.
passage:
Consider three cases of causation. A motorist runs a red light and injures a cyclist; a factory owner poisons a stream and kills the fish; a builder uses poor materials and a wall collapses. In each case the but-for test is satisfied straightforwardly: but for the defendant's act, the injury would not have occurred. One might generalise: an act is the cause of an injury if and only if the injury would not have occurred but for the act, the act was a necessary condition of the injury, and no other sufficient cause existed.

Now consider a different scenario. Two hunters are shooting in a field, each acting independently and without any connection to the other. Each fires negligently in the direction of a bystander who is struck and injured. Investigation reveals that either shot, taken alone, would have been sufficient to cause the injury sustained. The but-for test, applied to each hunter individually, yields a paradoxical result: the injury would have occurred even if hunter A had not fired, because hunter B's shot was independently sufficient; and vice versa. Neither act is a necessary condition of the injury. Another sufficient cause existed in each case — the other hunter's shot. All three conditions of the proposed definition fail for each defendant. If the definition is correct, neither hunter caused the injury, and the bystander — undeniably injured by the negligence of two persons — is without remedy against either. The law rejects this absurdity. In cases of overdetermination, where two independently sufficient causes operate simultaneously to produce an indivisible injury, the law abandons the but-for test and asks instead whether each defendant's act was a substantial factor in bringing about the harm. Both hunters are held liable as concurrent tortfeasors. The background cases, with their tidy single-cause structure, presented a misleading picture of causal simplicity, and the definition, built upon that picture, cannot accommodate the more complex but equally real phenomenon of causal overdetermination.

background_cases: A manufacturer of motor cars sells a vehicle with a defective braking system. The purchaser is injured when the brakes fail. The manufacturer is held liable for the defective product.
A manufacturer of electrical appliances sells a heater with faulty wiring. The purchaser's house catches fire. The manufacturer is held liable.
A manufacturer of tinned food sells a tin containing a harmful contaminant. The purchaser falls ill. The manufacturer is held liable.
definition: A manufacturer is liable for harm caused by a defective product if and only if (i) the manufacturer sold the product directly to the person injured, (ii) the defect existed at the time of manufacture, and (iii) the defect caused the harm complained of.
conditions: the manufacturer sold the product directly to the person injured
the defect existed at the time of manufacture
the defect caused the harm complained of
counterexample: A manufacturer sells a bottle of ginger beer to a retail shop. The shopkeeper sells it to a customer, who shares it with a friend. The friend discovers the remains of a decomposed snail in the bottle and falls ill. The manufacturer did not sell the product to the friend; the manufacturer sold it to the retailer, who sold it to the customer, who shared it with the friend. The first condition fails entirely. Yet the manufacturer is liable to the friend, for the law has established that a manufacturer owes a duty of care to all persons who may foreseeably consume or use the product, regardless of whether they purchased it from the manufacturer or indeed purchased it at all.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved purchasers who bought directly from the manufacturer, and the definition required a direct sale between manufacturer and injured party. But these cases were drawn from a context in which the chain of distribution happened to be short. The law has moved beyond the requirement of privity of contract in product liability, recognising that manufacturers owe a duty of care to the ultimate consumer and to all persons who may foreseeably be affected by the product, regardless of the contractual chain through which the product passed. The definition's insistence on a direct sale reflected the structure of the sample cases, not the structure of the law.
passage:
Consider three cases of product liability. A motor car with defective brakes injures its purchaser; a heater with faulty wiring causes a fire in its purchaser's home; a contaminated tin of food makes its purchaser ill. In each case the manufacturer sold the product directly to the person injured, the defect existed at the time of manufacture, and the defect caused the harm. One might define manufacturer's liability accordingly: a manufacturer is liable for harm caused by a defective product if and only if the manufacturer sold the product directly to the person injured, the defect existed at the time of manufacture, and the defect caused the harm.

But suppose a manufacturer of bottled beverages sells a consignment to a wholesale distributor, who sells it to a retail shop, who sells individual bottles to the public. A customer purchases a bottle and shares it with a friend. The friend pours the contents into a glass and discovers that the bottle contains a foreign substance that renders the drink harmful. The friend falls ill. The manufacturer did not sell the bottle to the friend; the chain of distribution interposes three intermediaries. The friend did not even purchase the bottle; she received it as a gift, so to speak, from the customer. The first condition of the proposed definition is not satisfied. Yet the manufacturer is liable. The development of the law of negligence, in a landmark decision concerning precisely such facts, established that a manufacturer who puts a product into circulation in a form intended to reach the ultimate consumer without intermediate inspection owes a duty of care to that consumer, and by extension to anyone who may foreseeably use or consume the product. The privity requirement — the insistence upon a direct contractual relationship — was swept away because it produced injustice: it permitted manufacturers to shelter behind the distribution chain whilst the persons actually harmed by their negligence were left without remedy. The background cases, with their simple two-party structure, obscured this difficulty, and the definition built upon them inherited their limitation.

background_cases: A boxer enters a prizefight, consenting to the blows he may receive. He is struck and bruised. No action in battery lies, for he consented to the contact.
A rugby player takes the field, consenting to the tackles and collisions inherent in the game. He suffers a sprained ankle. No action lies, for he consented to the risks of the sport.
A fencer participates in a bout, consenting to the thrust and parry of the foils. She receives a bruise from a touch. No action lies, for she consented to the contact involved.
definition: A person's consent to physical contact in the course of a sporting activity is valid, and bars any action for battery, if and only if (i) the person voluntarily participates in the activity, (ii) the contact is of a kind ordinarily incident to the activity, and (iii) the person has been informed of the specific risks of injury before participating.
