conditions: the person voluntarily participates in the activity
the contact is of a kind ordinarily incident to the activity
the person has been informed of the specific risks of injury before participating
counterexample: A schoolboy joins a game of football in the playground during the luncheon interval. No one informs him of the specific risks of injury; he simply joins in, as boys do. During the game he is tackled and suffers a minor sprain. He was not informed of the specific risks before participating — indeed, no one could have identified them in advance — yet the contact was of a kind ordinarily incident to the game, and his participation was voluntary. The third condition fails, but consent is nonetheless implied from the voluntary participation in an activity whose risks are common knowledge. No action for battery lies.
abductive_insight: The background cases involved organised sporting contests — a prizefight, a rugby match, a fencing bout — in which participants typically receive some form of briefing or at least have the opportunity to learn of the risks in advance. The definition abstracted from this a requirement of specific prior information. But the background cases were all formal, organised events, and the requirement of prior information was a feature of their institutional setting, not a necessary condition of valid consent to sporting contact. In informal and spontaneous sporting activities, consent is implied from the act of voluntary participation itself, and the law does not require that the participant have been specifically informed of risks that are matters of common understanding. The definition mistook a procedural feature of organised sport for a universal condition of consent.
passage:
Consider three cases of consent to sporting contact. A boxer consents to the blows of a prizefight; a rugby player consents to the tackles of a match; a fencer consents to the touches of a bout. Each participates voluntarily, the contact is incident to the activity, and each has been informed, formally or informally, of the risks involved. From these cases one might define valid consent to sporting contact: a person's consent is valid, and bars any action for battery, if and only if the person voluntarily participates, the contact is ordinarily incident to the activity, and the person has been informed of the specific risks of injury before participating.

Now consider a schoolboy who, during the luncheon interval, joins a game of football in the playground with his classmates. No one sits him down and explains the risks of sprained ankles, bruised shins, or collisions. He simply sees the game in progress and runs to join it, as schoolboys have done since time immemorial. During the game he is tackled, falls awkwardly, and sprains his wrist. The third condition of the proposed definition is not satisfied: no one informed him of the specific risks of injury before he began to play. Yet it would be absurd to say that his consent to the ordinary physical contacts of a playground football game was invalid, or that an action for battery might lie against the boy who tackled him. The schoolboy consented by the very act of joining the game. The risks of minor injury in a game of football are matters of the most commonplace knowledge; one need not be informed of them to be taken to have accepted them. The background cases were all drawn from organised, formal sporting contests, where institutional procedures typically include some communication of risks. But the majority of sporting activity in everyday life is spontaneous and informal, and consent to its ordinary incidents is implied from voluntary participation without any requirement of prior specific information. The definition extrapolated a feature of formal sport into a universal requirement and thereby excluded the most common and natural form of sporting consent.

background_cases: A farmer sells a horse to a dealer at a country fair, the price is agreed by word of mouth, the horse is inspected and handed over, and the transaction is complete within the hour.
A merchant sells a quantity of cloth to a draper across the counter of his shop, the draper pays in coin, the cloth is wrapped and carried away, and the bargain is concluded in a single encounter.
A vintner sells a cask of wine to a tavern-keeper, the cask is rolled out of the cellar, the price is paid in silver, and both parties depart satisfied within the space of an afternoon.
definition: A promissory exchange E constitutes a binding contract if and only if (i) E involves the transfer of a specific, identifiable thing from one party to another, (ii) E is concluded in a single encounter in which both parties are physically present, and (iii) E is accompanied by the simultaneous exchange of the thing for its price.
conditions: E involves the transfer of a specific, identifiable thing from one party to another
E is concluded in a single encounter in which both parties are physically present
E is accompanied by the simultaneous exchange of the thing for its price
counterexample: A ship-owner in Liverpool contracts with a merchant in Boston for the carriage of goods across the Atlantic. The contract is formed by an exchange of letters; the parties have never met and are separated by three thousand miles of ocean. No specific, identifiable thing is transferred at the moment of formation — the ship has not yet sailed, the goods have not yet been loaded, and the freight will not be paid until the cargo is delivered. Yet the contract is binding; each party may sue the other for its breach.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all spot transactions — the sale of a horse, the purchase of cloth, the buying of wine — in which a specific thing changed hands in a single face-to-face encounter with simultaneous payment. The definition abstracted these features as essential to contractual obligation because they were present in every case examined. But the cases were drawn exclusively from immediate, in-person exchanges of goods for money. The shipping contract reveals that binding obligations may arise between persons who have never met, through correspondence conducted across vast distances, in respect of performances that lie entirely in the future. The definition mistook the features of the simplest form of commercial transaction for the necessary conditions of contractual obligation as such.
passage:
Consider three transactions that are plainly binding contracts. A farmer sells a horse to a dealer at a country fair: the horse is inspected, the price is agreed by word of mouth, the money is handed over, and the horse is led away. A merchant sells a quantity of cloth to a draper across the counter of his shop: the draper examines the fabric, names a price, pays in coin, and carries the cloth home. A vintner sells a cask of wine to a tavern-keeper: the cask is tapped and tasted, the price is settled, the money changes hands, and the cask is rolled away. From these cases one might extract a definition: a promissory exchange constitutes a binding contract if and only if it involves the transfer of a specific, identifiable thing, is concluded in a single encounter with both parties physically present, and is accompanied by the simultaneous exchange of the thing for its price. The three cases satisfy all three conditions admirably.

But one must consider the shipping contract. A ship-owner in Liverpool writes to a merchant in Boston, offering to carry a consignment of manufactured goods from Liverpool to Boston at a stated rate of freight, the goods to be loaded within thirty days and the freight to be paid upon delivery. The merchant writes back accepting the offer. A binding contract has been formed. Yet no specific, identifiable thing has been transferred; the goods have not yet been loaded, the ship has not yet sailed, and the freight is to be paid only upon delivery some weeks hence. The contract was not concluded in a single encounter; it was formed through an exchange of letters, written and posted days apart, across three thousand miles of ocean. The parties have never met; the ship-owner has never seen the merchant, nor the merchant the ship-owner. And there was no simultaneous exchange of thing for price; every performance under the contract lies in the future. Yet the obligation is real and legally enforceable. The definition, drawn from cases of immediate, face-to-face, spot transactions, mistook the features of that particular form of exchange for the necessary conditions of contractual obligation, when in truth contracts may be formed at a distance, by correspondence, between strangers, in respect of performances that are wholly executory.

background_cases: A blacksmith owns his forge, his anvil, and his hammers — tools he fashioned with his own hands or purchased with his earnings, which he uses daily in the practice of his trade.
A farmer owns his plough, his cart, and his draught-horses — implements acquired through years of labour and saving, essential to the cultivation of his fields.
A weaver owns her loom, her shuttles, and her store of yarn — instruments of her craft, acquired by purchase or inheritance, upon which her livelihood depends.
definition: A person P has a property right over a thing T if and only if (i) T is a tangible, physical object that P can take into his possession and hold, (ii) P makes regular and productive use of T, and (iii) the deprivation of T would directly impair P's ability to earn a livelihood.
conditions: T is a tangible, physical object that P can take into his possession and hold
P makes regular and productive use of T
the deprivation of T would directly impair P's ability to earn a livelihood
counterexample: Consider the property right of an author over the manuscript of a novel he has written. The manuscript is, to be sure, a tangible object — ink upon paper — but the author's property right is not over the paper and ink; it is over the composition, the arrangement of words, the work of the imagination. This is an intangible right — a right that subsists in the products of the mind rather than in any physical thing that can be held in the hand. The author may not make regular use of the manuscript; he may have finished the novel and set it aside. And its deprivation might not impair his livelihood — he might earn his bread by other means. Yet his property in the literary composition is real, and the law protects it.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all instances of ownership over tangible tools of trade — the blacksmith's forge, the farmer's plough, the weaver's loom — objects that are physical, regularly used, and essential to earning a living. The definition abstracted tangibility, regular use, and livelihood-dependence as the essential marks of property. But the cases were drawn from a narrow domain of artisanal tool-ownership, and the definition mistook these features for the features of property rights in general. The author's right over his literary composition reveals that property may subsist in intangible creations of the mind, may be held without regular use, and may exist independently of any connexion to the owner's livelihood. The definition confused one familiar species of property — the craftsman's right over his tools — with property as such.
passage:
Consider three cases of undoubted property rights. A blacksmith owns his forge, his anvil, and his hammers — tools he fashioned with his own hands or purchased with his earnings, which he uses every working day. A farmer owns his plough, his cart, and his draught-horses — implements essential to the cultivation of his fields, acquired through years of labour and saving. A weaver owns her loom, her shuttles, and her yarn — the instruments upon which her livelihood depends. From these cases one might extract a definition: a person has a property right over a thing if and only if the thing is a tangible physical object that can be taken into possession and held, the person makes regular and productive use of it, and its deprivation would directly impair the person's ability to earn a livelihood.

But one must consider the author and his manuscript. A man has spent three years composing a novel. The manuscript sits upon his desk — sheets of paper covered in his handwriting. The paper and ink are tangible, to be sure, but the author's property right is not over the paper and ink as such; it is over the literary composition — the arrangement of words, the plot, the characters, the expression of the author's imagination. This right is intangible; it cannot be held in the hand, weighed upon a scale, or locked in a chest. Another man might copy out the very same words upon fresh paper, and the author's right would be violated even though his physical manuscript remained untouched. The author may not make regular use of the manuscript; having completed the novel, he may not look at it for months. And the deprivation of the right might not impair his livelihood — he might earn his bread as a schoolmaster, and write only in his leisure hours. Yet his property in the composition is real, is recognised by the law, and may be enforced against any person who reproduces, distributes, or performs the work without his consent. The definition, drawn from cases of tangible, productive, livelihood-essential property, mistook the features of the craftsman's right over his tools for the essential features of property, and thereby failed to accommodate the important class of rights that subsist in the intangible products of the human mind.

background_cases: A coachman drives at reckless speed through a village, his horses galloping out of control, and strikes a pedestrian crossing the road ahead of him.
A workman carrying a heavy beam upon his shoulder turns carelessly in a narrow lane and strikes a passer-by who was walking behind him.
A householder, repairing his roof, allows a tile to slip from his hand; it falls into the street below and injures a man standing directly beneath.
definition: A person P is negligent towards a person Q if and only if (i) P's careless act directly and physically contacts Q or an object in Q's immediate possession, (ii) Q was within P's field of vision at the time of the careless act, and (iii) the harm to Q followed immediately — within seconds — upon P's act.
conditions: P's careless act directly and physically contacts Q or an object in Q's immediate possession
Q was within P's field of vision at the time of the careless act
the harm to Q followed immediately — within seconds — upon P's act
counterexample: A chemist carelessly allows a toxic substance to drain from his workshop into a stream that flows through several parishes. A farmer three miles downstream waters his cattle from the stream; the cattle sicken and die. The chemist's act did not directly contact the farmer or anything in his immediate possession — the poison travelled three miles through flowing water. The farmer was not within the chemist's field of vision; indeed, the chemist did not know of the farmer's existence. And the harm did not follow immediately; it manifested days later, after the cattle had drunk repeatedly from the contaminated stream. Yet the chemist was negligent towards the farmer.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all instances of immediate, visible, physically direct harm — the coachman's horses striking a pedestrian, the workman's beam hitting a passer-by, the tile falling upon a bystander. The definition abstracted direct physical contact, visibility, and temporal immediacy as essential to negligence. But the cases were drawn from a narrow class of face-to-face, instantaneous accidents, and the definition mistook their features for the features of negligence in general. The poisoned stream reveals that negligence may operate at a distance, upon persons unknown and unseen by the wrongdoer, through a medium — flowing water — that carries the harmful agent far from its source, and with a temporal lag of days rather than seconds. The definition confused the features of immediate physical negligence with the necessary conditions of negligent liability as such.
passage:
Consider three cases that are plainly instances of negligence. A coachman drives at reckless speed through a village, his horses galloping out of control, and strikes a pedestrian who was crossing the road in plain sight. A workman carrying a heavy beam turns carelessly in a narrow lane and strikes a man walking behind him. A householder, mending his roof, allows a tile to slip from his grasp; it falls to the street below and strikes a man standing directly beneath. From these cases one might extract a definition: a person is negligent towards another if and only if his careless act directly and physically contacts the other or something in the other's immediate possession, the injured party was within his field of vision, and the harm followed immediately — within seconds — upon the act.

But one must consider the chemist and the poisoned stream. A chemist maintains a workshop in which he processes various substances for commercial sale. Through carelessness — a failure to maintain the drains, a neglected stopper, an oversight in the disposal of waste — he allows a toxic compound to seep from his premises into a small stream that flows past his workshop and winds through several parishes of open country. Three miles downstream, a farmer waters his cattle at this same stream, as he has done for years. Over the course of several days the cattle sicken; within a fortnight, half the herd is dead. The chemist's careless act did not directly contact the farmer or anything in his immediate possession; the poison passed through three miles of flowing water, traversing several properties and parishes, before reaching the farmer's cattle. The farmer was not within the chemist's field of vision; the chemist did not know of the farmer's existence, had never seen his pastures, and was wholly unaware that cattle drank from the stream at that distance. And the harm did not follow within seconds; it manifested gradually over days and weeks. Yet the chemist was negligent towards the farmer. His carelessness set in motion a chain of consequences that, through the natural medium of the watercourse, reached and injured a person whom he had never seen. The definition, drawn from cases of immediate, visible, direct harm, mistook these features for the essential conditions of negligence, and thereby failed to accommodate the important class of cases in which negligence operates at a distance, through intermediary media, upon persons unknown to the wrongdoer.

background_cases: A master is held liable for injury caused by his servant who, whilst driving the master's cart on the master's business, carelessly runs down a pedestrian.
A factory owner is held liable for injury to a passer-by caused by a defective machine operated by one of his employees during the ordinary course of work.
A ship-owner is held liable for damage to cargo caused by the negligence of his crew during a voyage undertaken on his instructions and for his profit.
definition: A person P is vicariously liable for harm caused by another person Q if and only if (i) Q is P's employee or servant, acting within the scope of his employment, (ii) P has the right to control the manner in which Q performs his work, and (iii) the harm occurred whilst Q was carrying out a task that P had specifically instructed him to perform.
conditions: Q is P's employee or servant, acting within the scope of his employment
P has the right to control the manner in which Q performs his work
the harm occurred whilst Q was carrying out a task that P had specifically instructed him to perform
counterexample: Consider the case of the owner of a motor-car who lends it to a friend for an afternoon's excursion. The friend, through careless driving, injures a pedestrian. The friend is not the owner's employee or servant; he is an independent person, borrowing the car as a favour. The owner has no right to control the manner of the friend's driving. And the friend was not carrying out a task specifically instructed by the owner; he was driving for his own pleasure. Yet in certain circumstances the law may hold the owner liable — on the ground that he entrusted a dangerous instrument to another and is answerable for the consequences of that entrustment.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all instances of the classic master-servant relationship — the cart-driver, the factory worker, the ship's crew — in which the employer directed the work and had the right to control its execution. The definition abstracted employment, control, and specific instruction as essential to vicarious liability. But the cases were drawn from the narrow domain of employer-employee relations, and the definition mistook their features for the necessary conditions of vicarious liability in all its forms. The lending of the motor-car reveals that liability for another's acts may arise from the entrustment of a dangerous thing, without any employment relationship, without the right to control the other's conduct, and without any specific instruction as to the task performed. The definition confused the conditions of respondeat superior with the general conditions under which one person may be answerable for the harm caused by another.
passage:
Consider three cases in which a person is held liable for harm caused by another. A master sends his servant to deliver goods by cart; the servant drives carelessly and injures a pedestrian. The master is held liable, for the servant was acting within the scope of his employment, and the master had the right to direct and control the manner of his work. A factory owner is held liable when one of his operatives, working a machine in the ordinary course of his duties, causes injury to a passer-by through negligent operation. A ship-owner is held liable for damage to cargo caused by the negligence of his crew during a voyage undertaken on his orders and for his profit. From these cases one might extract a definition: a person is vicariously liable for harm caused by another if and only if the other is his employee, he has the right to control the manner of the work, and the harm occurred during a specifically instructed task.

But one must consider the lending of the motor-car. A man possesses a powerful motor-car and lends it for an afternoon to a friend who wishes to drive into the country. The friend is not the owner's employee or servant; he is an independent person, borrowing the vehicle as an act of friendship. The owner has no right to control the manner of the friend's driving; having handed over the keys, he has relinquished control of the vehicle entirely. And the friend is not carrying out any task instructed by the owner; he is driving for his own amusement, to a destination of his own choosing. Yet the friend drives carelessly and injures a pedestrian. In certain circumstances — particularly where the motor-car is recognised as an inherently dangerous instrument, and the owner has entrusted it to a person without enquiring into his competence — the law may hold the owner liable for the consequences of the friend's negligence. The owner placed a dangerous thing in the hands of another, and the entrustment itself may ground a species of liability that has nothing to do with employment, control, or specific instruction. The definition, drawn from the classic master-servant cases, mistook the conditions of respondeat superior for the general conditions under which one person may be answerable for the harm caused by another, and thereby overlooked the important principle that the entrustment of a dangerous instrument may, of itself, generate liability.

background_cases: A man attacked by a robber on a highway draws his sword and wounds his assailant, using no more force than is needed to repel the attack and secure his escape.
A woman assaulted in her home by an intruder strikes him with a fire-iron, using only such force as the emergency requires to drive him from the house.
A shopkeeper, threatened with a knife by a thief, seizes a weighted stick from behind the counter and strikes the thief's arm to disarm him.
definition: A person P acts in lawful self-defence if and only if (i) P is defending his own body against an assault upon his person, (ii) P uses a physical instrument — a weapon or tool — to repel the attack, and (iii) the force P uses is directed solely at the assailant's body.
conditions: P is defending his own body against an assault upon his person
P uses a physical instrument — a weapon or tool — to repel the attack
the force P uses is directed solely at the assailant's body
counterexample: A mother, seeing a stranger seize her young child by the arm and attempt to drag the child away, throws herself upon the stranger, wrestling the child free with her bare hands and pushing the stranger to the ground. She is not defending her own body; she is defending her child. She uses no physical instrument; she acts with her hands alone. And the force she directs is not solely at the stranger's body — she also grasps and pulls the child. Yet her act is plainly one of lawful defence.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all instances of personal self-defence using instruments — the sword against the robber, the fire-iron against the intruder, the stick against the thief — in which the defender protected his own body by directing an instrument at the assailant. The definition abstracted defence of one's own person, use of a physical instrument, and force directed solely at the assailant as essential conditions. But the cases were drawn from a narrow class of armed, personal self-defence. The mother defending her child reveals that lawful defence may extend to the protection of another person, may be conducted with bare hands rather than instruments, and may involve force that is directed partly at the person being rescued. The definition confused the features of one paradigmatic form of self-defence with the necessary conditions of lawful defensive force in all its forms.
passage:
Consider three cases of lawful self-defence. A traveller set upon by a robber on a lonely highway draws his sword and wounds the robber, using only such force as is needed to repel the attack and make his escape. A woman, attacked in her own home by a man who has forced his way through the door, seizes a fire-iron from the hearth and strikes the intruder, using only the force that the emergency demands. A shopkeeper, confronted by a thief brandishing a knife, reaches for a weighted stick behind the counter and brings it down upon the thief's arm, disarming him. From these cases one might extract a definition: a person acts in lawful self-defence if and only if he is defending his own body against an assault, uses a physical instrument to repel the attack, and directs the force solely at the assailant's body.

But one must consider the mother and the child. A woman is walking with her young daughter in a public park when a stranger approaches, seizes the child by the arm, and begins to pull the child away. The mother, without hesitation, throws herself upon the stranger. She grasps the child with one hand and pushes the stranger with the other; she wrestles and struggles, pulling the child free and forcing the stranger to the ground. Her action is not the defence of her own body — she herself has not been assaulted, and no blow has been aimed at her. She uses no physical instrument — no weapon, no stick, no iron bar; she acts with her bare hands, relying on nothing but her own strength and the ferocity of maternal instinct. And the force she employs is not directed solely at the stranger's body; much of her physical effort is directed at the child, whom she grasps, pulls, and lifts to safety. Yet her act is plainly lawful. No reasonable person would deny that the mother has a right to defend her child, and no court would censure her for the means she employed. The definition, drawn from cases of personal, instrumental self-defence, mistook the features of that particular form for the necessary conditions of all lawful defensive force, and thereby failed to accommodate the defence of others, the use of bare hands, and the complex physical interactions that attend the rescue of a third party from an assailant.

background_cases: A merchant offers to sell a quantity of cloth at a stated price; a buyer accepts the offer and pays the price; both parties perform their respective obligations, and neither disputes that a binding contract exists.
A landlord offers to let a house for a term of years at a stated rent; a tenant accepts, pays the rent, and takes possession; the agreement is reduced to writing and signed by both parties.
A shipowner agrees to carry goods from London to Calcutta for a stated freight; the merchant delivers the goods and pays the freight; the shipowner transports the goods as agreed.
definition: A binding contract exists between two parties if and only if (i) one party makes an offer that is accepted by the other, (ii) each party furnishes consideration — that is, each gives or promises something of value, and (iii) the terms of the agreement are sufficiently certain to be enforced by a court.
conditions: one party makes an offer accepted by the other
each party furnishes consideration
the terms are sufficiently certain to be enforced
counterexample: Consider the case of a father who promises his adult daughter that he will pay her an annual allowance of two hundred pounds if she will refrain from bringing any legal proceedings against him in respect of certain family disputes. The daughter accepts the offer and refrains from litigation. There is an offer and an acceptance; there is consideration, for the daughter forbears from a legal right she is entitled to exercise; and the terms are certain. Yet one might question whether a binding contract has been formed, for the agreement is made within the domestic sphere — between father and daughter — and the courts have consistently held that agreements between family members are presumed not to be intended to create legal relations. The three conditions are satisfied, yet the agreement may not be enforceable. The definition has omitted a fourth condition: the intention to create legal relations.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all commercial transactions — sales of goods, leases of property, contracts of carriage — in which the intention to create legal relations was so obvious as to be invisible. No one doubts that a merchant who agrees to sell cloth at a stated price intends the agreement to be legally binding. The definition, abstracted from these commercial paradigms, captured offer, acceptance, consideration, and certainty of terms, but overlooked the intention to create legal relations because that intention was uniformly present and therefore unnoticed. The domestic agreement — between father and daughter, concerning family affairs — reveals the omission. In the domestic sphere, the intention to create legal relations cannot be presumed; it must be established. The definition mistook the commercial case, in which intention is always present, for the general case, and thereby omitted a condition that is essential to the formation of a binding contract.
passage:
Consider three cases of binding agreement. A merchant offers to sell a quantity of cloth at a stated price; a buyer accepts the offer and pays; both parties perform their obligations, and the existence of a binding contract is beyond question. A landlord offers to let a house for a term of years; a tenant accepts, pays the rent, and takes possession; the agreement is reduced to writing and signed. A shipowner agrees to carry goods from London to Calcutta for a stated freight; the merchant delivers the goods and pays; the shipowner performs the carriage as agreed. In each case, there is an offer and an acceptance, each party furnishes consideration, and the terms are certain. From these cases one might extract a definition: a binding contract exists if and only if there is an offer accepted by the other party, each party furnishes consideration, and the terms are sufficiently certain to be enforced.

But one must consider the case of a father who promises his adult daughter that he will pay her an annual allowance of two hundred pounds if she will refrain from bringing any legal proceedings against him in respect of certain family disputes. The daughter accepts the offer and refrains from litigation. There is an offer — the father's promise of the allowance — and an acceptance — the daughter's agreement to forbear. There is consideration on both sides: the father promises money; the daughter forbears from the exercise of a legal right. The terms are certain — two hundred pounds per annum, payable in consideration of forbearance from specified litigation. Every condition of the definition is satisfied. Yet the courts have held, in a long and well-established line of authority, that agreements between members of a family are presumed not to be intended to create legal relations. The domestic context of the agreement — the relationship of father and daughter, the subject-matter of family disputes, the informal circumstances of the promise — raises a presumption that the parties did not intend to bind themselves legally. The three conditions of the definition are met, but the agreement may not constitute a binding contract. The background cases were all commercial transactions — the sale of cloth, the letting of a house, the carriage of goods — in which the intention to create legal relations was so obvious as to require no mention. The definition, abstracted from commercial paradigms in which that intention was uniformly and silently present, failed to identify it as a distinct condition. The domestic agreement reveals the gap: where the context does not support the presumption of legal intention, the satisfaction of offer, acceptance, consideration, and certainty of terms does not suffice to create a binding contract.

background_cases: A farmer encloses a tract of waste land, cultivates it, and uses it openly and without interruption for twenty years; at the end of that period he is recognised as the owner by prescription.
A squatter occupies an abandoned cottage in the countryside, repairs it, and lives in it openly and continuously for twenty years; the law recognises his title by long possession.
A fisherman uses a stretch of riverbank openly and without challenge for twenty years, mooring his boat and drying his nets there; his right to use the land is established by prescription.
definition: A person P acquires title to land by prescription if and only if (i) P has been in continuous and uninterrupted possession of the land for the requisite statutory period, (ii) P's possession has been open and notorious — that is, visible to the world and not concealed, and (iii) P's possession has been adverse to the interests of the true owner — that is, without the true owner's permission.
conditions: P has been in continuous and uninterrupted possession for the statutory period
P's possession has been open and notorious
P's possession has been adverse — without the true owner's permission
counterexample: Consider a tenant who occupies a house under a lease for twenty-one years. His possession is continuous and uninterrupted; it is open and notorious — the whole neighbourhood knows he lives there. Yet his possession is not adverse to the true owner's interests; he occupies the house with the owner's permission, under a lease, and pays rent. The three conditions of the definition, taken at face value, are satisfied with respect to continuity and openness, but the third condition — adversity — fails. However, one may construct a more troubling case. Suppose a person occupies land in the genuine but mistaken belief that he is the true owner — because, for example, a boundary has been incorrectly drawn upon a map. His possession is continuous, open, and adverse in the sense that he does not have permission from the true owner. Yet he does not regard his possession as adverse; he believes himself to be the owner. The definition, which speaks of possession 'adverse to the interests of the true owner,' captures this case only if adversity is understood objectively — as the absence of permission — rather than subjectively, as a hostile intent. The definition has omitted the requirement that the possessor must treat the land as his own, which is a further condition not reducible to the mere absence of permission.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved persons who occupied land knowing it was not theirs and acting in open defiance of or indifference to the true owner's rights — the farmer who enclosed waste land, the squatter who moved into an abandoned cottage, the fisherman who used another's riverbank. In each case, the adversity of the possession was both objective (no permission) and subjective (the possessor treated the land as his own). The definition abstracted continuity, openness, and adversity as the essential conditions but treated adversity simply as the absence of permission, overlooking the further requirement that the possessor must treat the land as his own. The case of the person who possesses land under a mistaken belief of ownership reveals the gap: his possession is without permission, and thus 'adverse' in the narrow sense, but the full analysis of prescriptive title requires that he have treated the land as his own — a condition that was silently satisfied in every background case but that the definition, drawn from those cases, failed to articulate as a distinct requirement.
passage:
Consider three cases in which a person acquires title to land by long possession. A farmer encloses a tract of waste land, ploughs it, and uses it openly for twenty years; at the end of that period he is recognised as the owner. A squatter occupies an abandoned cottage, repairs it, and lives in it openly and continuously for twenty years; his title is established. A fisherman uses a stretch of another man's riverbank, mooring his boat and drying his nets there without concealment or challenge for twenty years; his right is recognised. In each case, the possession is continuous, open, and adverse — that is, without the true owner's permission. From these cases one might extract a definition: a person acquires title by prescription if and only if his possession is continuous for the statutory period, open and notorious, and adverse to the true owner's interests.

But one must consider a more troubling case. Suppose a man occupies a strip of land adjoining his own property in the genuine but mistaken belief that it is part of his holding. A boundary has been incorrectly marked upon the ordnance survey, and the man, relying upon the map, treats the strip as his own. He fences it, cultivates it, and maintains it for the requisite period. His possession is continuous and uninterrupted. It is open and notorious; the whole parish can see that he uses the strip. And it is adverse in the sense that he does not have the true owner's permission; the true owner, who lives at a distance, is unaware of the encroachment. The three conditions of the definition appear to be satisfied. Yet the character of this man's possession differs from that of the farmer who knowingly enclosed another's waste land. The farmer treated the land as his own in the knowledge that it was not; his possession was an assertion of right against the true owner. The man who relies upon the faulty map also treats the land as his own, but without any consciousness of adversity; he does not know he is encroaching. The background cases were all instances in which the possessor knowingly asserted dominion over another's land — cases in which the adversity of the possession included both the absence of permission and the possessor's treatment of the land as his own. The definition captured the absence of permission but failed to articulate the further condition that the possessor must treat the land as his own — a condition that is distinct from the mere absence of permission and that the law requires for the running of the prescriptive period.

background_cases: A motorist drives at excessive speed through a residential area and strikes a pedestrian who is crossing the road; the pedestrian suffers broken bones and is awarded damages.
A factory owner fails to fence a dangerous machine, and a worker's hand is caught in the mechanism; the worker is injured and recovers compensation.
A shopkeeper spills oil upon the floor of his premises and fails to clean it up; a customer slips, falls, and breaks her hip; she is awarded damages for the shopkeeper's negligence.
definition: A person P is liable in negligence for a harm H suffered by a person Q if and only if (i) P owes a duty of care to Q, (ii) P breaches that duty by failing to take the precautions that a reasonable person would take, and (iii) P's breach causes H — that is, H would not have occurred but for P's breach.
conditions: P owes a duty of care to Q
P breaches that duty by failing to take reasonable precautions
P's breach causes H — H would not have occurred but for P's breach
counterexample: Consider the case of a physician who negligently misdiagnoses a patient's condition. The patient has a disease that, even with correct diagnosis and the best available treatment, would have resulted in the same outcome — severe disability. The physician owes a duty of care; the physician breaches that duty by misdiagnosing; yet the patient would have suffered the same harm regardless of the breach, because no available treatment would have altered the outcome. The third condition fails under a strict but-for analysis: the harm would have occurred even without the breach. Yet one feels that the physician's negligence is not without consequence, for the patient has been deprived of the chance — however small — of a better outcome.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all instances in which the defendant's negligence was clearly the but-for cause of the plaintiff's injury. Had the motorist driven at a reasonable speed, he would not have struck the pedestrian. Had the factory owner fenced the machine, the worker's hand would not have been caught. Had the shopkeeper cleaned the floor, the customer would not have slipped. In every case, the but-for test yielded a clear and unambiguous result. The definition, abstracted from these cases, adopted the but-for test as the criterion of causation. But the background cases were all ones in which the causal link was straightforward and uncontested. The case of the negligent misdiagnosis reveals the limitation: when the harm would have occurred regardless of the breach, the but-for test fails, yet the physician's negligence has deprived the patient of the chance of a better outcome. The definition, drawn from cases of clear causation, could not accommodate the more subtle causal structure of lost-chance cases, in which the defendant's negligence does not cause the harm but diminishes the prospect of avoiding it.
passage:
Consider three cases of negligence in which liability is straightforward. A motorist drives at excessive speed through a residential area and strikes a pedestrian; the pedestrian would not have been struck had the motorist driven at a reasonable speed. A factory owner fails to fence a dangerous machine; a worker's hand is caught in the mechanism; the injury would not have occurred had the machine been fenced. A shopkeeper spills oil upon the floor and fails to clean it up; a customer slips and is injured; she would not have fallen had the floor been clean. In each case, the defendant owes a duty of care, breaches it, and the breach is the but-for cause of the harm. From these cases one might extract a definition: a person is liable in negligence if and only if he owes a duty of care, breaches it, and the breach is the but-for cause of the harm.

But one must consider the case of a physician who negligently misdiagnoses a patient's condition. The patient presents with symptoms that, correctly interpreted, would indicate a rare and aggressive disease. The physician, through want of care, misinterprets the symptoms and provides an incorrect diagnosis. By the time the error is discovered, the disease has progressed beyond the reach of effective treatment. The patient suffers severe disability. The physician owes a duty of care to the patient; that is not in dispute. The physician breaches that duty by misdiagnosing the condition; a competent and careful physician would have identified the disease correctly. But would the patient have avoided the harm had the diagnosis been correct? The evidence suggests that the disease was of a kind for which the available treatments offer, at best, a modest chance of success. Even with a correct and timely diagnosis, the probability of a favourable outcome was no more than one in four. The patient would, more probably than not, have suffered the same disability regardless of the physician's negligence. The but-for test fails: on the balance of probabilities, the harm would have occurred even without the breach. Yet the physician's negligence has deprived the patient of a chance — a real, if modest, chance — of a better outcome. The definition, drawn from cases in which the causal link between breach and harm was direct and unambiguous, could not accommodate the case in which the defendant's negligence diminishes the prospect of avoiding harm without being, on the balance of probabilities, its but-for cause.

background_cases: A man punches another in the face, intending to cause harm; the victim suffers a broken jaw; the assailant is held liable for the injury.
A driver runs a red signal at a road junction and collides with another vehicle; the other driver is injured; the first driver is held liable for negligence.
A landowner stores explosives upon his land without adequate precaution; the explosives detonate and damage a neighbour's property; the landowner is held liable.
definition: A person P is liable for a harm H if and only if (i) P's act or omission is a cause of H, (ii) P was at fault — that is, P acted intentionally, recklessly, or negligently, and (iii) the harm was of a kind that was reasonably foreseeable at the time of P's conduct.
conditions: P's act or omission is a cause of H
P was at fault — intentional, reckless, or negligent
the harm was reasonably foreseeable
counterexample: Consider the manufacturer of a patent medicine who sells a preparation that is contaminated with a harmful substance. The contamination is not the result of any negligence in the manufacturing process; the manufacturer has taken every reasonable precaution, and the contamination arises from an undetectable defect in the raw materials supplied by a reputable supplier. A consumer takes the medicine and is made seriously ill. The manufacturer's act — selling the medicine — is a cause of the harm. But the manufacturer was not at fault; he was neither intentional, nor reckless, nor negligent. The harm was not reasonably foreseeable, since the defect was undetectable by any known method. The second and third conditions fail. Yet the law may hold the manufacturer liable under the doctrine that a person who puts a product into circulation impliedly warrants its fitness, and that the manufacturer of a defective product bears responsibility for the harm it causes regardless of fault.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all instances of fault-based liability — a deliberate assault, a negligent driver, a careless landowner — in which the defendant was personally culpable and the harm was foreseeable. The definition, abstracted from these cases, treated fault and foreseeability as necessary conditions of liability. But the background cases were drawn from the domain of personal wrongdoing, in which the defendant's own culpable conduct was the source of the harm. The case of the manufacturer who sells a contaminated product without fault reveals the limitation: the law imposes liability upon manufacturers not because they are at fault but because they have put a product into circulation and have thereby assumed responsibility for its safety. The definition, shaped by paradigms of fault-based liability, could not accommodate the principle of product liability, in which the defendant's responsibility arises from his role in the chain of distribution rather than from any personal culpability.
passage:
Consider three cases of legal liability. A man punches another in the face, intending to cause injury; the victim's jaw is broken; the assailant is liable. A driver runs a red signal and collides with another vehicle; the other driver is injured; the first driver is liable for negligence. A landowner stores explosives without adequate precaution; the explosives detonate and damage a neighbour's property; the landowner is liable. In each case, the defendant's act is a cause of the harm, the defendant was at fault, and the harm was of a kind that was reasonably foreseeable. From these cases one might extract a definition: a person is liable for a harm if and only if his act or omission causes the harm, he was at fault, and the harm was reasonably foreseeable.

But one must consider the manufacturer of a patent medicine. The manufacturer produces a preparation in accordance with the highest standards of the trade. His factory is clean, his processes are sound, his ingredients are obtained from reputable suppliers, and every batch is tested before it is released for sale. Nevertheless, a particular batch is contaminated with a harmful substance — a contaminant that was present in the raw materials and that could not have been detected by any test known to the manufacturer or to the science of his time. A consumer purchases the preparation, takes it according to the directions, and is made seriously ill. The manufacturer's act — the sale of the medicine — is a cause of the harm; had he not sold the contaminated preparation, the consumer would not have been injured. But the manufacturer was not at fault in any sense that the definition recognises. He was not negligent; his precautions were exemplary. He was not reckless; he had no reason to suspect contamination. He acted without any culpable mental state whatsoever. Nor was the harm reasonably foreseeable; the contamination was undetectable. Yet the law may hold the manufacturer liable. The doctrine that a person who puts a product into the stream of commerce impliedly warrants its safety has a long lineage, and the principle that a manufacturer bears responsibility for defective products regardless of fault has been gaining ground in the jurisprudence of many jurisdictions. The definition, drawn from cases of personal wrongdoing in which fault and foreseeability were always present, could not accommodate the distinct principle that liability may attach to the manufacturer of a defective product not because he is personally culpable but because he has introduced the product into circulation and has thereby assumed a responsibility that transcends personal fault.

background_cases: A patient reads a detailed consent form describing a surgical procedure, its risks, and its alternatives, and signs the form before the operation; the consent is valid.
A man agrees to participate in a boxing match, understanding that he may receive blows to the body and face; his consent to the risk of injury is valid.
A woman agrees to the terms of a contract after reading and understanding every clause; her consent to the contractual obligations is valid.
definition: A person P gives valid consent to an act A if and only if (i) P is informed of the material facts concerning A, (ii) P understands those facts, and (iii) P agrees to A voluntarily, without coercion or undue influence.
conditions: P is informed of the material facts concerning A
P understands those facts
P agrees voluntarily, without coercion or undue influence
counterexample: Consider the case of a person who consents to a medical procedure after being informed of all material facts, who understands those facts, and who agrees voluntarily. But the information is communicated in a manner that, whilst technically complete, is designed to minimise the appearance of risk — the physician emphasises the benefits in vivid and reassuring language whilst reciting the risks in rapid, technical jargon that, although understood in a literal sense, does not convey their true gravity. The patient consents. He has been informed; he understands the words; he is not coerced. Yet his consent, though it satisfies all three conditions, is defective, for the manner of disclosure has been calculated to produce agreement rather than to facilitate genuine understanding. The three conditions are met, yet the consent is not the product of the autonomous deliberation the definition is meant to protect.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all instances in which the information was communicated straightforwardly and in good faith — a detailed consent form, the understood rules of a boxing match, a contract read clause by clause. The definition, abstracted from these cases, treated information, understanding, and voluntariness as sufficient for valid consent. But the background cases were ones in which the manner of disclosure was unproblematic, and the definition therefore did not need to address the quality of the communicative process. The case of the physician who discloses all material facts but frames them so as to minimise the appearance of risk reveals the gap: consent may be formally informed, understood, and voluntary, yet substantively defective because the manner of disclosure has been manipulated to produce a particular outcome. The definition, drawn from cases of straightforward disclosure, could not accommodate the possibility that the process of informing may itself be a vehicle of influence.
passage:
Consider three cases in which consent is plainly valid. A patient reads a detailed consent form describing a surgical procedure, its risks, and its alternatives; he understands the form and signs it freely. A man agrees to participate in a boxing match, understanding that he will receive blows and may be injured; his consent to the physical contact is valid. A woman reads and understands every clause of a contract and agrees to its terms without pressure; her consent is beyond question. In each case, the person is informed of the material facts, understands them, and agrees voluntarily. From these cases one might extract a definition: a person gives valid consent if and only if he is informed of the material facts, understands them, and agrees voluntarily.

But one must consider a more troubling case. A patient consults a physician about a proposed surgical procedure. The physician discloses all the material facts — the nature of the procedure, its benefits, its risks, and the available alternatives. No fact is withheld. The patient understands the words spoken; he is an educated man, familiar with medical terminology. He is not coerced; no one threatens or pressures him. Yet the physician's manner of disclosure is carefully calibrated. The benefits of the procedure are described in vivid, concrete, and reassuring language: 'Most patients return to full activity within a fortnight.' The risks are recited in rapid, technical jargon: 'There is a two per cent incidence of post-operative haematoma formation and a point-five per cent risk of anastomotic dehiscence.' The information is technically complete and literally understood, but the manner of its presentation — the warmth and expansiveness with which the benefits are described, the briskness and opacity with which the risks are dispatched — is designed to produce agreement. The patient consents. He has been informed of the material facts; he understands them in a literal sense; he agrees voluntarily. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied. Yet one may doubt whether this consent reflects the autonomous deliberation that the requirement of informed consent is meant to protect. The manner of disclosure has shaped the patient's decision as surely as any omission of fact would have done, yet the definition, drawn from cases in which the communicative process was straightforward and undertaken in good faith, has no resources to identify the defect. It captures the content of disclosure but not its quality — the manner, the emphasis, the framing — and thereby permits a species of manipulation that is fully compatible with the formal satisfaction of its conditions.

background_cases: A buyer and a seller negotiate the terms of a sale, agree upon a price, and the buyer pays in exchange for the goods; the agreement is a binding contract supported by mutual consideration.
A landlord offers to let a flat at a stated rent; a tenant accepts and pays the first month's rent; the tenancy agreement is a binding contract, with consideration flowing in both directions.

A publisher agrees to pay a royalty to an author in exchange for the right to print and sell the author's manuscript; the agreement is a contract, with each party providing consideration to the other.
definition: An agreement between two parties is a binding contract if and only if (i) there is an offer by one party and an acceptance by the other, (ii) there is consideration — each party provides something of value, and (iii) the consideration is adequate — that is, the value provided by each party is roughly proportionate to the value received.
conditions: there is an offer and an acceptance
there is consideration
the consideration is adequate — the values exchanged are roughly proportionate
counterexample: Consider a collector of rare books who agrees to sell a first edition of immense value to a friend for one pound. The collector makes the offer; the friend accepts; the friend pays one pound and receives the book. There is an offer and an acceptance. There is consideration: the friend gives one pound, the collector gives the book. But the consideration is grossly inadequate — one pound for a book worth thousands. Condition (iii) fails. Yet the agreement is a binding contract. The law of contract does not, in general, enquire into the adequacy of consideration. Provided that something of value is exchanged — however trifling — the courts will enforce the agreement. The principle is that parties are free to make whatever bargains they choose, and the court will not substitute its own judgement of value for theirs.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the sale, the tenancy, the publishing agreement — all involved exchanges in which the consideration was roughly proportionate: a fair price for goods, a market rent for a flat, a reasonable royalty for a manuscript. The definition abstracted adequacy of consideration as a necessary condition because, in every background case, the values exchanged were approximately balanced. But the background cases were drawn from ordinary commercial transactions in which rough proportionality is the norm, and the definition mistook this contingent feature of the examples for a legal requirement. The book sold for one pound reveals that the law requires only the presence of consideration, not its adequacy, and that parties are free to strike bargains that an outsider might regard as grossly unequal.
passage:
Consider three agreements that are paradigmatically binding contracts. A buyer and a seller negotiate the sale of a horse. They agree upon a price that reflects the horse's market value; the buyer pays the price and receives the horse. There is an offer, an acceptance, and consideration: money for a horse, each roughly proportionate to the other. A landlord offers to let a flat at a rent that reflects the market rate; a tenant accepts and pays the first month's rent. Again, the consideration is roughly proportionate — the rent bears a reasonable relation to the value of the accommodation. A publisher agrees to pay a royalty to an author in exchange for the right to print the author's work; the royalty reflects the expected sales, and the exchange is approximately balanced. From these cases one might extract a definition: an agreement is a binding contract if there is an offer and acceptance, if there is consideration, and if the consideration is adequate — that is, the values exchanged are roughly proportionate.

But one must consider the case of a collector of rare books who agrees to sell a first edition of great value — a book worth many thousands of pounds — to a friend for one pound. The collector makes the offer freely; the friend accepts without hesitation; the friend tenders one pound and receives the book. There is an offer and an acceptance. There is consideration: the friend gives one pound, the collector gives the book. But the consideration is grossly disproportionate. One pound for a book worth thousands is not, by any standard, adequate consideration. Condition (iii) fails. Yet the agreement is, in law, a binding contract. The courts have long held that they will not enquire into the adequacy of consideration. Provided that something of value passes from each party to the other — however nominal, however disproportionate — the exchange constitutes sufficient consideration to support a contract. The principle is one of freedom of contract: parties are at liberty to make whatever bargains they choose, and the law will not intervene merely because one party has made a poor deal. The background cases involved transactions in which the consideration happened to be roughly proportionate, and the definition abstracted this proportionality as a legal requirement. The book sold for one pound reveals that the law demands the presence of consideration but not its adequacy, and that a definition which insists upon rough proportionality will exclude a vast class of contracts that the law enforces without hesitation.

background_cases: A motorist who drives at excessive speed in a residential area and strikes a pedestrian is negligent; he owed a duty of care, his driving fell below the standard of a reasonable motorist, and his negligence caused the pedestrian's injury.

A surgeon who leaves a sponge inside a patient's body after an operation is negligent; she owed a duty of care, her conduct fell below the standard of a competent surgeon, and the retained sponge caused infection and harm.

A factory owner who fails to fence dangerous machinery, with the result that a worker's hand is caught in the mechanism, is negligent; he owed a duty of care, his failure fell below the standard of a reasonable factory owner, and the failure caused the worker's injury.
definition: A person P is negligent with respect to a harm H if and only if (i) P owed a duty of care to the person harmed, (ii) P's conduct fell below the standard of care that a reasonable person would have exercised, and (iii) P's breach of the duty of care was the immediate and direct cause of H, with no intervening factors.
conditions: P owed a duty of care
P's conduct fell below the reasonable standard
P's breach was the immediate and direct cause of H with no intervening factors
counterexample: Consider a building contractor who negligently constructs a wall with inferior materials. The wall stands for five years without incident. Then a severe but not extraordinary storm strikes the area, and the wall collapses, injuring a passer-by. The contractor owed a duty of care to those who might be affected by his work. His use of inferior materials fell below the standard of a competent builder. But the storm was an intervening factor between the contractor's negligence and the harm. It was the storm, not the contractor's hand, that immediately and directly caused the wall to fall. Condition (iii) fails. Yet the contractor is negligent. The storm was a foreseeable event — storms occur in this region with some regularity — and the wall should have been built to withstand them. The contractor's negligent construction was the underlying cause of the collapse, and the storm was merely the occasion upon which the latent defect manifested itself. The definition's requirement of immediate and direct causation, with no intervening factors, would exculpate the contractor — and every other negligent builder whose defective work fails only when subjected to foreseeable stress.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the speeding motorist, the negligent surgeon, the factory owner — were all instances in which the defendant's negligence directly and immediately caused the harm, with no intervening event between the breach and the injury. The definition abstracted this immediate causal connection as essential. But the background cases were drawn from situations in which cause and effect were closely linked in time and space, and the definition mistook this proximity for a necessary condition of negligent causation. The collapsing wall reveals that negligence may cause harm through a chain of events that includes foreseeable intervening factors — such as weather, normal use, or the passage of time — and that the law traces causation through such factors so long as they were foreseeable, rather than requiring that the defendant's breach be the immediate and unmediated cause of the harm.
passage:
Consider three cases of negligence. A motorist drives at excessive speed through a residential area and strikes a pedestrian. The causal connection is direct and immediate: the motorist's speeding was the proximate cause of the impact, with no intervening event between the breach and the harm. A surgeon leaves a sponge inside a patient during an operation. The retained sponge causes infection — again, the causal connection is direct, with no intervening factor between the negligent act and the resulting harm. A factory owner fails to fence dangerous machinery, and a worker's hand is caught in the mechanism. The failure and the injury are directly connected, with nothing intervening. From these cases one might extract a definition: a person is negligent if the person owed a duty of care, the person's conduct fell below the reasonable standard, and the breach was the immediate and direct cause of the harm with no intervening factors.

But one must consider a building contractor who constructs a wall using inferior materials — materials that a competent builder would not have used. The wall is erected and stands for five years without incident. Then a storm strikes the area — a severe storm, to be sure, but not one of extraordinary or unprecedented violence; storms of this intensity occur in the region every few years. The wall, weakened by its inferior construction, collapses under the force of the wind, and the falling masonry injures a passer-by. The contractor owed a duty of care to those who might foreseeably be affected by his work. His use of inferior materials fell below the standard of a competent builder. But the storm was an intervening factor between the contractor's negligence and the harm. It was not the contractor's hand that pushed the wall over; it was the wind. The collapse occurred five years after the negligent act, and the storm was the immediate trigger. Condition (iii) fails. Yet the contractor is negligent, and the law holds him liable. The storm was foreseeable — it was not an event of such extraordinary character as to break the chain of causation. The wall should have been built to withstand foreseeable weather, and its failure to do so was a consequence of the contractor's negligent choice of materials. The background cases involved situations in which cause and effect were closely linked, with no intervening event, and the definition abstracted this immediacy as a necessary condition. The collapsing wall reveals that negligent causation may operate through a chain of events that includes foreseeable intervening factors, and that the law traces responsibility through such factors rather than requiring that the defendant's breach be the last link in the chain before the harm.

background_cases: A man poisons another's drink; the victim consumes the drink and dies. The poisoner's act is the cause of death — but for the poisoning, the victim would not have died.

A driver runs a red light and collides with another vehicle; the occupant of the other vehicle is injured. The driver's act is the cause of the injury — but for the running of the light, the collision would not have occurred.

A factory discharges waste into a river; downstream residents fall ill from contaminated water. The discharge is the cause of the illness — but for the discharge, the water would not have been contaminated.
definition: An act A is the cause of a harm H if and only if (i) but for A, H would not have occurred, (ii) A was a necessary condition of H, and (iii) A was sufficient by itself to produce H — that is, no other conditions were needed in conjunction with A.
conditions: but for A, H would not have occurred
A was a necessary condition of H
A was sufficient by itself to produce H
counterexample: Consider a case in which two hunters, acting independently, fire simultaneously at a bird. Both shots miss the bird but strike a bystander, and either shot alone would have been sufficient to cause the injury the bystander sustains. Now consider a different structure: a man negligently leaves a campfire burning in a forest. A strong wind — a normal and foreseeable occurrence in the season — fans the embers into a blaze that spreads to a neighbouring property and burns a barn. The man's act — leaving the fire — was a necessary condition of the harm: but for the unattended fire, the barn would not have burned. But the act was not sufficient by itself to produce the harm. The fire would not have spread without the wind, without the dry conditions, without the proximity of the barn. Multiple conditions — the wind, the drought, the geographical proximity — were needed in conjunction with the man's act to produce the harm. Condition (iii) fails. Yet the man is the cause of the fire in law; his negligence set the chain of events in motion, and the contributing conditions were natural and foreseeable, not extraordinary interventions that would break the causal chain.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the poisoning, the collision, the discharge — were all instances in which the defendant's act appeared to be not merely necessary but sufficient to produce the harm, because the contributing conditions — the victim's consumption of the drink, the presence of the other vehicle, the downstream residents' use of the water — were so ordinary and so taken for granted that they were invisible. The definition abstracted sufficiency as a condition of causation because the background cases concealed the role of contributory factors. The campfire reveals that no act is truly sufficient by itself to produce a harm — every harm results from a conjunction of conditions — and that the law identifies one condition as 'the cause' not because it was sufficient alone but because it was the condition that was wrongful, avoidable, and salient, whilst the other conditions were normal, expected, and unremarkable.
passage:
Consider three cases in which an act is identified as the cause of a harm. A man poisons another's drink, and the victim dies. The poisoning was the cause of death: but for the introduction of poison, the victim would not have died. A driver runs a red light and collides with another vehicle, injuring its occupant. The running of the light was the cause of the injury: but for the driver's violation, the collision would not have occurred. A factory discharges toxic waste into a river, and downstream residents fall ill. The discharge was the cause of the illness: but for the contamination, the water would have been safe. In each case, the defendant's act appears to be not merely necessary but sufficient to produce the harm — the poisoning killed, the red-light violation caused the crash, the discharge contaminated the water. One might extract a definition: an act is the cause of a harm if and only if but for the act the harm would not have occurred, the act was a necessary condition, and the act was sufficient by itself to produce the harm.

But one must consider a man who negligently leaves a campfire burning in a forest during a dry season. The fire smoulders for some hours. Then a strong wind — the kind of wind that blows regularly in this region during the season — fans the embers into a blaze. The fire spreads through the dry undergrowth, crosses a boundary, and burns a neighbour's barn to the ground. The man's act — leaving the campfire unattended — was a necessary condition of the harm: but for the fire, the barn would not have burned. But the act was not sufficient by itself. The fire would not have spread without the wind. The wind would not have driven the fire to the barn without the dry conditions. The barn would not have been reached without its geographical proximity to the forest. Multiple conditions — meteorological, environmental, geographical — combined with the man's negligence to produce the harm, and no one of them, including the man's act, was sufficient alone. Condition (iii) fails. Yet the man is the cause of the fire in law. His negligence set in motion a chain of events that, in conjunction with foreseeable natural conditions, produced the harm. The law does not require that the defendant's act be the sole sufficient cause; it requires that the act be a substantial and operative cause, and that the contributing conditions be of the ordinary, foreseeable kind rather than extraordinary interruptions. The background cases appeared to involve sufficient causes because the contributing conditions — the victim's drinking, the presence of the other car, the residents' use of the water — were so normal as to be invisible. The campfire reveals that every harm results from a conjunction of conditions, and that the law singles out one condition as 'the cause' not because it was alone sufficient but because it was the condition that was wrongful and avoidable, whilst the others were merely the ordinary circumstances of life.

background_cases: When a buyer offers to purchase goods at a stated price and the seller accepts, the agreement is enforced because each party has made a voluntary commitment, each has provided consideration, and each understood the terms.

When an employer offers a position with specified salary and conditions and the employee accepts, the contract is enforced because both parties freely consented to definite terms and each provided consideration — labour for wages.

When a landlord offers a lease specifying rent, duration, and conditions of occupancy, and the tenant accepts, the agreement is enforced because each party has made a knowing and voluntary commitment supported by consideration.

When a borrower promises to repay a sum lent by a creditor at a specified rate of interest, the agreement is enforced because both parties have freely committed to specific terms and each has provided consideration.
definition: An agreement between parties P and Q is enforceable as a contract if and only if (i) there has been a clear offer by one party and an unequivocal acceptance by the other, (ii) each party has provided consideration — something of value given or promised in exchange, and (iii) the terms of the agreement are sufficiently definite to be enforced.
conditions: Clear offer and unequivocal acceptance
Each party has provided consideration
The terms are sufficiently definite
counterexample: Consider an agreement between two business partners to share equally the profits of a venture they are about to undertake. One partner offers, the other accepts. Each provides consideration — labour, capital, and expertise. The terms are definite: equal sharing of profits. All three conditions are met. But the venture is the smuggling of prohibited goods across an international border. The agreement, though it satisfies the formal requirements of contract formation, is unenforceable because its purpose is illegal. No court will lend its authority to the enforcement of an agreement whose performance requires the commission of a crime.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved agreements whose subject-matter was lawful — the sale of goods, employment, tenancy, lending. The definition captures the formal requirements of contract formation but drops the implicit condition that the agreement's purpose and performance must be lawful. An agreement that satisfies all the formal conditions but whose subject-matter is illegal or contrary to public policy is unenforceable, and the definition does not account for this.
passage:
The agreements that the law most readily enforces share a common structure. The sale of goods, the employment contract, the lease, the loan — in each, one party has made a clear offer, the other has accepted, consideration has been exchanged, and the terms are sufficiently definite to be given legal effect. From these cases, one might extract the definition: an agreement is enforceable if and only if there has been a clear offer and acceptance, consideration has been provided by both parties, and the terms are definite.

Yet this definition, abstracted from cases whose subject-matter was invariably lawful, fails to capture a condition that was silently present in every background case. Consider two partners who agree to share equally the profits of a smuggling operation. One makes a clear offer; the other accepts without reservation. Each provides consideration — one contributes capital, the other provides expertise and labour. The terms are definite: equal division of profits. All three conditions are satisfied. But no court will enforce this agreement, because its subject-matter is the commission of a crime. The law will not lend its authority to an arrangement whose performance requires illegality. The background cases involved the sale of lawful goods, lawful employment, lawful tenancy, and lawful lending; their lawfulness was so obvious that it did not need to be stated. But the definition, by omitting it, generates the false conclusion that any agreement satisfying the formal requirements is enforceable, regardless of its purpose. What is missing is a condition requiring that the agreement's subject-matter and purpose be lawful and not contrary to public policy.

background_cases: When a motorist runs a red light and strikes a pedestrian, the motorist's action is the cause of the pedestrian's injuries: but for the motorist's failure to stop, the collision would not have occurred.

When a manufacturer sells a product with a defective component and the component fails, injuring the user, the defect is the cause of the injury: but for the defect, the product would have functioned safely.

When a physician prescribes the wrong medication and the patient suffers an adverse reaction, the prescription is the cause of the harm: but for the erroneous prescription, the patient would not have been exposed to the wrong drug.
definition: An act A is the cause of a harm H if and only if (i) H would not have occurred but for A, (ii) A preceded H in time, and (iii) there is a continuous chain of physical events linking A to H.
conditions: H would not have occurred but for A
A preceded H
A continuous chain of physical events links A to H
counterexample: Consider a factory that discharges a chemical into a river over many years. Downstream, the residents of a town develop an elevated rate of a particular cancer.
