Epidemiological evidence strongly supports a causal connection between the chemical and the cancer, but no continuous chain of physical events from the discharge to any individual's cancer can be traced — the mechanism by which the chemical causes cancer is statistical rather than deterministic, operating through probabilistic interactions at the cellular level. Condition (i) may be satisfied in a probabilistic sense: but for the discharge, the cancer rate would have been lower. Condition (ii) is satisfied: the discharge preceded the cancers. But condition (iii) — a continuous chain of physical events linking the discharge to the harm — is not satisfied in the way the definition demands, because the causal mechanism is statistical rather than deterministic. Yet the residents' claim is one that the law should recognise.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved direct, mechanistic causation: a collision, a component failure, a chemical reaction. The definition abstracts from these cases a requirement of continuous physical linkage, but many genuine causal connections — particularly those involving toxic exposure, statistical causation, and cumulative harm — operate through probabilistic mechanisms that cannot be traced as a continuous chain from act to injury. The definition captures deterministic, mechanistic causation but excludes probabilistic and statistical causation.
passage:
The clearest cases of legal causation involve direct, mechanistic connections. The motorist runs the red light and strikes the pedestrian; the defective component fails and injures the user; the wrong medication produces an adverse reaction. In each case, a continuous chain of physical events links the defendant's act to the plaintiff's injury, and the but-for test is straightforwardly satisfied. From these cases, one might extract the definition: an act is the cause of a harm if the harm would not have occurred but for the act, the act preceded the harm, and a continuous chain of physical events connects them.

Yet this definition, modelled on cases of direct mechanical causation, encounters difficulties when applied to a large and important class of harms. Consider a factory that discharges a toxic chemical into a river for decades. The residents of a downstream town develop cancer at rates significantly above the baseline. Epidemiological studies establish a strong statistical association between exposure to the chemical and the cancer. But no one can trace a continuous chain of physical events from the discharge to any particular resident's tumour. The mechanism is probabilistic: the chemical increases the probability of cellular mutation, but whether any given cell mutates is a matter of chance. The but-for test is satisfied in a statistical sense — but for the discharge, the cancer rate would have been lower — and temporal priority is established. But the requirement of a continuous physical chain, drawn from the paradigm of mechanical causation, is not met. The background cases involved billiard-ball causation — one event directly producing the next — but toxic-exposure cases involve probabilistic mechanisms that the definition does not accommodate. What is wanted is a broader account of causation that recognises statistical and probabilistic connections, not merely deterministic chains.

background_cases: A building contractor who negligently installs faulty wiring is liable when the wiring causes a fire that damages the client's property, because the contractor's negligence created an unreasonable risk of the very kind of harm that occurred.
A road authority that fails to repair a known pothole is liable when a motorist is injured by driving into it, because the authority's failure to act created an unreasonable risk to road users.
A hospital that negligently allows a patient to fall from a bed is liable for the patient's injuries, because the hospital's failure to provide adequate safeguards created the risk that materialised.
definition: A defendant D is liable for harm suffered by a plaintiff P if and only if (i) D owed a duty of care to P, (ii) D breached that duty by creating an unreasonable risk of harm, and (iii) the harm that P suffered was of the type that D's breach made more probable.
conditions: D owed a duty of care to P
D breached that duty by creating an unreasonable risk
The harm was of the type made more probable by D's breach
counterexample: Consider a landlord who negligently fails to install adequate fire escapes in an apartment building. A fire breaks out — caused not by the landlord's negligence but by a tenant's carelessness with a candle — and a resident, unable to escape due to the absence of fire escapes, suffers smoke inhalation and is seriously injured. The landlord owed a duty of care to the residents. The landlord breached that duty by failing to install fire escapes, thereby creating an unreasonable risk of harm in the event of fire. And the harm — injury from inability to escape a fire — was precisely the type of harm that the absence of fire escapes made more probable. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the fire itself was caused by a third party's negligence, and one might argue that the landlord's liability should be reduced or shared. The definition attributes full liability to the landlord without addressing the role of the tenant's independent negligence in causing the fire.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved situations in which the defendant's negligence was the sole or dominant cause of the harm. The definition captures the duty-breach-harm structure but does not address situations of contributory or concurrent negligence — cases in which the plaintiff's harm results from the combined negligence of multiple parties, requiring apportionment of liability rather than full attribution to a single defendant.
passage:
The cases that most clearly illustrate the structure of liability share a common pattern. The negligent contractor created the risk that materialised; the negligent road authority failed to prevent a foreseeable danger; the negligent hospital failed to safeguard a vulnerable patient. In each case, the defendant owed a duty, breached it by creating an unreasonable risk, and the harm that followed was of the very kind the duty was designed to prevent. One might extract from these cases the definition: a defendant is liable if he owed a duty of care, breached it by creating an unreasonable risk, and the harm was of the type made more probable by the breach.

Yet this definition, modelled on cases in which the defendant's negligence was the sole or clearly dominant cause, encounters a difficulty when the harm results from the combined negligence of multiple parties. Consider a landlord who negligently fails to install fire escapes in an apartment building. A fire, caused by a tenant's carelessness with a candle, breaks out, and a resident is injured because the absence of fire escapes prevents her from escaping. The landlord owed a duty, breached it, and the harm — injury from inability to escape fire — was precisely the kind of harm the breach made more probable. All three conditions are satisfied, and the definition attributes liability to the landlord. But the fire itself was caused by the tenant's independent negligence, and the harm resulted from the concurrence of two acts of negligence: the landlord's failure to provide escape routes and the tenant's carelessness with fire. The background cases did not involve this kind of concurrence. The definition assigns liability to the landlord in full, without providing any mechanism for recognising the tenant's contribution or for apportioning responsibility between two negligent parties. What is needed is a condition or qualification addressing situations of concurrent negligence, in which the harm flows from the combined failures of multiple actors.

background_cases: A buyer offers to purchase a house for a stated price, and the seller accepts the offer in writing within the specified period. Both parties intend to be bound, consideration is present, and the written acceptance mirrors the offer. A valid contract is formed.

A merchant offers to supply goods at a stated price per unit, and the retailer accepts by signing the order form. The acceptance is unconditional and matches the terms of the offer. A valid contract is formed.

An employer offers a position to a candidate at a stated salary, and the candidate accepts orally. The acceptance corresponds to the terms offered, and both parties intend to be bound. A valid contract is formed.
definition: A contract is formed between an offeror O and an offeree A if and only if (i) O communicates an offer to A that specifies the essential terms of the proposed agreement, (ii) A communicates an unconditional acceptance of the offer to O, and (iii) consideration passes or is promised from each party to the other.
conditions: O communicates an offer specifying essential terms
A communicates an unconditional acceptance
Consideration passes or is promised from each party
counterexample: Suppose a wholesaler sends a catalogue to a retailer listing goods at stated prices. The retailer selects items from the catalogue and sends an order. The wholesaler does not respond but ships the goods. There is no unconditional acceptance communicated to the retailer before performance begins — the wholesaler simply acts. Condition (ii) appears unsatisfied, since no acceptance was communicated prior to shipment. Yet in commercial practice, and under many legal systems, a contract is formed by the wholesaler's conduct — the shipment of goods constitutes acceptance by performance.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve acceptance communicated by explicit verbal or written statement prior to performance. The counterexample reveals that acceptance may also be manifested through conduct, particularly in commercial contexts where the parties' behaviour demonstrates assent more clearly than words. The definition's insistence on communicated acceptance fails to accommodate acceptance by performance.
passage:
One might observe that in the standard cases of contract formation — the house purchase, the supply agreement, the employment offer — a clear pattern obtains. An offeror communicates an offer specifying essential terms, the offeree communicates an unconditional acceptance, and consideration is exchanged. From these cases, one might extract a definition: a contract is formed when an offer specifying essential terms is met by an unconditional acceptance and supported by consideration.

Yet one might construct a case which reveals that the definition's requirement of communicated acceptance is too rigid. Suppose a wholesaler distributes a catalogue listing goods at stated prices. A retailer selects items and sends an order. The wholesaler, without communicating any acceptance, simply ships the goods. No words of acceptance are spoken or written; the wholesaler's assent is manifested entirely through conduct. Condition (ii) — the communication of an unconditional acceptance — appears to be unsatisfied, since the wholesaler never communicates acceptance in the conventional sense. Yet in commercial practice, and under the law of most jurisdictions, a contract is formed at the point of shipment. The wholesaler's conduct constitutes acceptance by performance. What the background cases have in common is that acceptance takes the form of an explicit communicative act — a signed document, an oral statement. This led to the inclusion of a condition requiring communicated acceptance. But the counterexample reveals that acceptance may be manifested through conduct as well as through words, and that the law recognises performance as a valid mode of acceptance in appropriate circumstances. The definition, by requiring communicated acceptance, fails to accommodate this well-established principle.

background_cases: A farmer encloses a parcel of unowned land, cultivates it for many years, and is recognised by his neighbours and by the local authority as its owner. His continuous, open, and exclusive use of the land has given rise to a possessory title.

A prospector discovers a mineral deposit on unclaimed land, marks out the boundaries of a claim, and works the deposit continuously. The legal system recognises his claim as establishing ownership of the mineral rights.

A family occupies an abandoned cottage, repairs it, and lives in it openly and without challenge for a period exceeding the statutory limitation. The law recognises their occupation as having matured into ownership through adverse possession.
definition: A person P acquires ownership of a thing T by possession if and only if (i) P has exercised continuous and exclusive physical control over T for a period prescribed by law, (ii) P's possession has been open and notorious — that is, visible to others and not concealed, and (iii) P's possession has been without the consent of any prior owner.
conditions: P has exercised continuous and exclusive physical control over T for the prescribed period
P's possession has been open and notorious
P's possession has been without the consent of any prior owner
counterexample: Suppose a person occupies a plot of land openly and continuously for the prescribed period, and without the consent of the registered owner. However, the registered owner is a minor who lacks the legal capacity to take action to recover the land, and the statute of limitations is tolled during minority in the relevant jurisdiction. All three conditions are met from the occupier's perspective — his possession has been continuous, open, and unconsented — yet the law does not recognise his claim because the limitation period has not, in legal contemplation, begun to run.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve situations in which the prior owner was either non-existent or was a competent adult who failed to assert his rights during the running of the limitation period. The counterexample reveals that the definition presupposes the capacity of the prior owner to take action, and that the prescribed period of possession is not merely a calendar duration but a legal period that may be extended or tolled by the disability of the owner.
passage:
One might observe that the cases commonly cited as paradigms of acquisitive possession share a clear pattern. The farmer who encloses and cultivates unowned land, the prospector who claims and works a mineral deposit, and the family who occupies an abandoned cottage all exercise continuous and exclusive physical control over the thing in question, do so openly and notoriously, and act without the consent of any prior owner. From these cases, one might extract a definition: a person acquires ownership by possession if and only if the person has exercised continuous and exclusive physical control for the prescribed period, the possession has been open and notorious, and it has been without the prior owner's consent.

Yet one might construct a case which reveals an assumption concealed within the background cases. Suppose a person occupies a plot of land openly and continuously for twenty years without the consent of the registered owner. The jurisdiction prescribes a twenty-year limitation period for the recovery of land. All three conditions of the definition appear to be satisfied. However, the registered owner is a minor — a child of ten at the time the occupation began — and the law of the jurisdiction tolls the running of the limitation period during minority. The twenty years of occupation, though meeting the calendar requirement, do not constitute a complete legal period, because the clock did not begin to run until the owner reached the age of majority. The definition, strictly applied, would recognise the occupier's claim, but the law does not. What the background cases all had in common, and what the definition implicitly assumed, was that the prior owner was either absent (in the case of unowned land) or was a competent adult capable of asserting his rights. The counterexample reveals that the 'prescribed period' is not a mere calendar duration but a legal concept that is sensitive to the capacity of the person against whom time runs. A more adequate definition would need to specify that the prescribed period must have run without interruption and without tolling by disability.

background_cases: A motorist drives through a red light and strikes a pedestrian, breaking the pedestrian's leg. The but-for test is satisfied: but for the motorist's act, the pedestrian would not have been struck. The motorist's negligence is the cause of the injury.

A factory discharges toxic waste into a river, and downstream residents fall ill. But for the discharge, the residents would not have been exposed to the toxin. The factory's conduct is the cause of the illness.

A surgeon negligently severs an artery during an operation, and the patient bleeds to death. But for the surgeon's error, the patient would have survived. The surgeon's negligence is the cause of death.
definition: An act A is the legal cause of a harm H if and only if (i) H would not have occurred but for A, (ii) A was a wrongful act (a breach of duty or violation of law), and (iii) H was a reasonably foreseeable consequence of A.
conditions: H would not have occurred but for A
A was a wrongful act
H was a reasonably foreseeable consequence of A
counterexample: Suppose two hunters, acting independently, each negligently fire their shotguns in the direction of a third person. Both pellets strike the victim simultaneously, and either wound alone would have been fatal. The victim dies. For each hunter, the but-for test fails: the victim would have died even if that particular hunter had not fired, because the other hunter's shot was independently fatal. Condition (i) is not satisfied for either hunter. Yet both hunters acted wrongfully, and the victim's death was a foreseeable consequence of their negligence. It would be absurd to hold that neither hunter caused the death.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve single wrongful acts that are individually necessary for the harm. The but-for test works well in such cases because there is a single chain of causation running from the wrongful act to the harm. The counterexample, involving two independently sufficient causes, reveals that the but-for test is adequate only when causes operate singly, and must be supplemented or replaced by a test — such as the NESS test — that can accommodate concurrent sufficient causes.
passage:
One might observe that in the standard cases of legal causation — the motorist who runs a red light, the factory that discharges toxic waste, the surgeon who severs an artery — a common pattern obtains. In each case, the harm would not have occurred but for the defendant's wrongful act, and the harm was a reasonably foreseeable consequence of that act. The but-for test, combined with a foreseeability requirement, appears to capture the essential features of legal causation. From these cases, one might extract a definition: an act is the legal cause of a harm if and only if the harm would not have occurred but for the act, the act was wrongful, and the harm was reasonably foreseeable.

Yet one might construct a case which reveals a fundamental limitation of the but-for test. Suppose two hunters, acting independently and without knowledge of each other, each negligently discharge their firearms in the direction of a third person. Both projectiles strike the victim simultaneously, and each wound is independently fatal. The victim dies. For each individual hunter, the but-for test fails: the victim would have died even if that hunter had not fired, because the other's shot was lethal on its own. Condition (i) is therefore not satisfied for either hunter. Yet both acted wrongfully, and the death was a foreseeable consequence of each hunter's negligence. The definition, applied strictly, would absolve both hunters of causal responsibility for the death — an outcome that is manifestly unjust and contrary to the holdings of every jurisdiction that has considered analogous cases. What the background cases all had in common was a single causal chain running from a single wrongful act to a single harm. The but-for test is well suited to such cases, but it breaks down when two or more independently sufficient causes converge upon the same result. A more adequate account of legal causation must accommodate this possibility, perhaps by replacing the but-for test with the NESS test, which asks whether the act was a necessary element of a set of conditions that was itself sufficient to produce the harm.

background_cases: A contract for the sale of goods between a merchant and a buyer is enforceable because both parties are competent adults who have freely agreed to the terms, each providing something of value — goods for money.

A contract of employment between a company and a worker is enforceable because both parties have legal capacity, the terms have been freely agreed, and consideration flows in both directions — labour for wages.

A lease agreement between a landlord and a tenant is enforceable because both parties are legally competent, the terms are freely negotiated, and each party provides consideration — the landlord provides the premises, the tenant pays rent.
definition: A contract between parties A and B is enforceable if and only if (i) both A and B have legal capacity, (ii) the terms of the agreement have been freely assented to by both parties, and (iii) each party provides consideration.
conditions: Both parties have legal capacity
The terms are freely assented to
Each party provides consideration
counterexample: Consider a contract between two competent adults who freely agree that one will pay the other a sum of money in exchange for the other's undertaking to commit an assault upon a third person. Both parties have legal capacity, the terms are freely assented to without coercion or deception, and consideration flows in both directions — money for the promised assault. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the contract is unenforceable because its object is illegal; no court will enforce an agreement whose performance requires the commission of a crime.
abductive_insight: The background cases — sale of goods, employment, lease — all involve agreements whose objects are lawful. The definition abstracts capacity, free assent, and consideration from these cases but omits the requirement that the contract's purpose and performance be lawful. The criminal-contract case reveals that enforceability requires not merely the formal prerequisites of a valid agreement but the legality of its object.
passage:
The contracts most commonly enforced in common-law jurisdictions share a set of formal features that invite codification. A sale of goods is enforceable because both parties are competent, the terms are freely agreed, and consideration is provided on both sides. An employment contract is enforceable for the same reasons. A lease binds landlord and tenant because each has capacity, each has freely assented, and each provides something of value. One might survey these cases and conclude that legal capacity, free assent, and consideration are the necessary and sufficient conditions of enforceability.

One might therefore define an enforceable contract as one in which both parties have legal capacity, the terms are freely assented to, and each party provides consideration. This definition captures the paradigm cases faithfully. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when the object of the contract is unlawful. Suppose two adults, of full legal capacity and acting without coercion, agree that one will pay the other a substantial sum in exchange for the other's promise to commit an assault upon a named third party. The agreement is clear, the assent is free, and consideration flows in both directions. All three conditions of the definition are met. Yet no court would enforce this contract; its purpose is the commission of a crime, and the law will not lend its authority to the enforcement of criminal undertakings. The background cases all involved lawful objects — goods, labour, premises — and the definition, drawn from these cases, failed to include legality of purpose as a condition. The omission permits the definition to classify as enforceable an agreement that every legal system would refuse to enforce.

background_cases: A motorist who drives carelessly and strikes a pedestrian is liable in negligence because the duty of care, the breach, the causation, and the harm are all clearly established, and the pedestrian's injury is a direct and foreseeable consequence of the driver's carelessness.

A surgeon who operates on the wrong limb is liable in negligence because the duty to exercise reasonable skill, the breach of that duty, and the resulting harm are straightforwardly connected — the patient's injury follows directly from the surgeon's error.

A property owner who fails to clear ice from a public footpath adjacent to his property is liable in negligence because pedestrians foreseeably rely upon the safety of the path, and the owner's failure to clear the ice directly causes the pedestrian's fall.
definition: A person P is liable in negligence if and only if (i) P owed a duty of care to the plaintiff, (ii) P breached that duty, and (iii) the plaintiff suffered physical harm as a direct result of the breach.
conditions: P owed a duty of care
P breached that duty
The plaintiff suffered physical harm as a direct result
counterexample: Consider an accountant who negligently prepares a financial report for a client. The client relies upon the erroneous report, makes a disastrous investment, and suffers severe financial loss — but no physical harm. The accountant owed a duty of care, breached it through negligence, and the client suffered harm as a direct result of the breach. Yet the harm is purely financial. The third condition, as stated, requires physical harm, and this condition is not met. Nevertheless, courts in many jurisdictions recognise liability in negligence for pure economic loss in appropriate circumstances.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the motorist, the surgeon, the property owner — all involve physical harm: bodily injury from a collision, surgical error, or a fall. The definition abstracts 'physical harm' from these cases as a necessary condition, but the accountant case reveals that negligence liability extends, in appropriate circumstances, to pure economic loss, showing that the restriction to physical harm is a feature of the paradigm cases rather than a necessary element of the tort.
passage:
The cases most commonly used to illustrate negligence liability share a conspicuous feature: the harm suffered by the plaintiff is physical. The pedestrian struck by the careless motorist suffers bodily injury. The patient operated upon in error suffers physical damage. The person who slips on an icy footpath sustains broken bones. One might naturally suppose that physical harm is a necessary condition of negligence liability.

One might formalise this supposition as follows: a person is liable in negligence if she owed a duty of care, breached it, and the plaintiff suffered physical harm as a direct result. This definition captures the paradigm cases. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when the harm is purely financial. Consider an accountant who, through carelessness, prepares a grossly erroneous financial report for a client. The client, relying upon the report, invests heavily in a venture that proves worthless. The client suffers no physical injury — he is not struck, burned, or broken — but his financial loss is devastating. The accountant owed a duty of care, breached it through negligence, and the client's loss is a direct consequence of the breach. The first two conditions are met, but the third — physical harm — is not. Yet courts in a number of jurisdictions have recognised liability in negligence for pure economic loss, at least where the relationship between the parties is sufficiently proximate and the reliance upon the defendant's skill is foreseeable. The definition, drawn from cases of physical injury, mistakes a common feature of negligence claims for a necessary element, thereby excluding an important and well-established category of negligence liability.

background_cases: The ownership of a house is a paradigm case of a property right because the owner may use the house, exclude others from it, sell it, bequeath it, and modify it as she sees fit — her rights are comprehensive and, in principle, perpetual.

The ownership of a parcel of agricultural land is a paradigm case because the farmer may cultivate it, exclude trespassers, transfer it by sale or gift, and leave it to his heirs — the rights are broad and temporally unlimited.

The ownership of a chattel — a motor car, a painting, a piece of furniture — is a paradigm case because the owner may use, exclude, transfer, and destroy the thing at will, subject only to general laws prohibiting nuisance and waste.
definition: A person P has a property right in a thing X if and only if (i) P may use X as P sees fit, (ii) P may exclude others from X, (iii) P may transfer X to another, and (iv) P's rights in X are perpetual — they do not expire after a fixed period.
conditions: P may use X as P sees fit
P may exclude others from X
P may transfer X to another
P's rights are perpetual
counterexample: Consider a patent — a right granted to an inventor over an invention for a limited period, typically twenty years. The patent holder may use the invention, exclude others from making or selling it, and transfer the patent to another by assignment or licence. The first three conditions are met. But a patent expires after its statutory term; the fourth condition — perpetuity — is not satisfied. Yet a patent is universally regarded as a property right. It is bought, sold, mortgaged, and litigated as property.
abductive_insight: The background cases — houses, land, chattels — all involve rights that are, in principle, perpetual. The definition abstracts perpetuity as a necessary condition, but the patent case reveals that some of the most important property rights in modern law are temporally limited by design. The definition mistakes a feature of tangible property rights for a necessary condition of all property rights, excluding intellectual property rights that are central to contemporary legal and economic life.
passage:
The property rights most readily identified as paradigmatic in the common-law tradition are rights over tangible things that endure, in principle, for ever. The owner of a house holds rights that do not expire after a fixed period; she may use, exclude, transfer, and bequeath the property indefinitely. The owner of agricultural land holds rights of similarly unlimited duration. The owner of a chattel — a motor car, a painting — holds rights that persist until the thing is destroyed or transferred. One might naturally suppose that perpetuity is an essential feature of a property right.

One might formalise this as follows: a person has a property right in a thing if she may use it, exclude others from it, transfer it, and her rights are perpetual. This definition accommodates the paradigm cases of houses, land, and chattels. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when we consider property rights that are temporally limited by design. A patent grants its holder the exclusive right to make, use, and sell an invention for a specified period — typically twenty years. The patent holder may use the invention as she sees fit, exclude others from practising it, and transfer the patent to another by assignment. The first three conditions are satisfied. But the patent expires at the end of its statutory term; the fourth condition fails. Yet no one doubts that a patent is a property right. Patents are bought and sold, mortgaged and licensed, inherited and litigated as property. The background cases, by featuring tangible things with perpetual rights, led the definition to treat perpetuity as essential. But the enormous and growing importance of intellectual property — patents, copyrights, trade marks — shows that temporally limited rights may be genuine property rights, and that perpetuity is a characteristic of one important class of property rather than a defining feature of all property.

background_cases: A motorist who negligently collides with a cyclist commits a tort because the motorist owed a duty of care to road users, breached that duty by driving carelessly, and the cyclist suffered physical injury as a direct result — the chain of causation is clear and unbroken.

A manufacturer who sells a product with a latent defect commits a tort when the defect causes injury to a consumer, because the manufacturer owed a duty of care to those who would use the product, the defect constituted a breach, and the consumer's injury was a direct result.

A surgeon who negligently performs an operation commits a tort because the surgeon owed a duty of care to the patient, the negligent performance was a breach, and the patient's worsened condition was directly caused by the breach.

A building contractor who fails to comply with safety regulations, causing a wall to collapse and injure a passer-by, commits a tort because the contractor owed a duty to those foreseeably affected, the non-compliance was a breach, and the injury resulted directly from the collapse.
definition: A party A has committed a tort against a party B if and only if (i) A owed a duty of care to B, (ii) A breached that duty by failing to conform to the standard of care expected of a reasonable person, and (iii) B suffered a legally recognisable injury that was directly caused by A's breach.
conditions: A owed a duty of care to B
A breached the duty by failing to meet the reasonable-person standard
B suffered injury directly caused by the breach
counterexample: Consider a factory that, through negligent waste disposal, contaminates a river over a period of years. Residents downstream develop health problems, but no single act of disposal can be identified as the cause of any particular resident's illness — the harm is the cumulative result of years of low-level contamination, compounded by the contributions of other factories along the river. The factory owed a duty of care to the downstream residents. It breached that duty by disposing of waste negligently. The residents suffered injury. But the requirement that the injury be 'directly caused' by the breach is difficult to satisfy: the causal chain runs through years of incremental contamination, the contributions of multiple tortfeasors, and the complex aetiology of the illnesses in question. No single breach directly caused any particular injury.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the motorist, the manufacturer, the surgeon, the contractor — all involve a single identifiable act of negligence that produces a discrete, traceable injury through a clear causal chain. The definition captures duty, breach, and direct causation but cannot accommodate cases of cumulative or diffuse harm, where the injury results from the aggregate effect of many acts over time and no single act can be identified as the direct cause of any particular harm.
passage:
The torts most readily recognised in common-law jurisdictions share a structural simplicity that the standard definition faithfully captures. The negligent motorist strikes the cyclist; the defective product injures the consumer; the careless surgeon harms the patient; the non-compliant contractor causes the wall to collapse. In each case, a single act of negligence produces a discrete injury through a clear, traceable causal chain. The duty, the breach, and the causation are all identifiable and attributable.

One might define a tort as a breach of a duty of care that directly causes a legally recognisable injury. This definition accommodates the paradigm cases. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises in the context of environmental harm. A factory disposes of chemical waste into a river over a period of years, in quantities that, taken individually, are small but that accumulate in the river sediment and in the tissues of organisms. Residents downstream develop chronic health problems. The factory owed a duty of care to those foreseeably affected by its waste disposal. Its practices fell below the standard of a reasonable operator. The residents have suffered injury. But the causal connection between the factory's breach and any particular resident's illness is diffuse and indirect. The contamination accumulated over years. Other factories contributed to the river's pollution. The illnesses have complex causes in which the contamination is one factor among several. No single act of disposal directly caused any identified harm. The definition, built on the model of the discrete tort — one act, one injury, one causal chain — cannot accommodate the cumulative, diffuse, multi-source causation that characterises environmental harm. One might observe that the law of tort must extend beyond cases of direct, traceable causation if it is to address the harms produced by the aggregate effects of conduct that, taken individually, falls below the threshold of identifiable injury.

background_cases: A farmer who clears a field, ploughs it, tills the soil, and plants crops has a strong claim to ownership of the field, because his labour has transformed a piece of uncultivated land into a productive resource; the labour that created the value grounds the ownership.

A craftsman who takes raw wood and fashions it into a chair has a strong claim to ownership of the chair, because his skill and effort have converted a raw material into a useful object; the labour invested in the transformation is the basis of the property right.

A prospector who discovers a mineral deposit on unclaimed land and extracts the ore through arduous effort has a strong claim to the extracted ore, because the labour of discovery and extraction created a valuable resource from what was previously inert material.
definition: A person P has a right of ownership over an object O if and only if (i) P has mixed P's labour with O, thereby transforming O or bringing O into a useful condition, (ii) O was not previously owned by another person, and (iii) P's appropriation of O leaves 'enough and as good' for others.
conditions: P has mixed labour with O, transforming it
O was not previously owned
P's appropriation leaves enough and as good for others
counterexample: Consider a person who, walking along a stretch of unowned coastline, collects a quantity of driftwood that has washed ashore. She expends minimal labour — she merely picks it up. She has not transformed it or brought it into a useful condition; the sea and the tides have already deposited it conveniently on the shore. The driftwood was not owned. The coastline is abundant, and plenty of driftwood remains for others. Conditions (ii) and (iii) are satisfied, but condition (i) — the mixing of labour in a transformative sense — is barely met, if at all. Yet it seems clear that the person who gathered the driftwood has a legitimate claim to it: to deny her ownership on the ground that she has not 'transformed' it would be to deny ownership in every case of simple gathering, which is one of the most ancient and intuitively legitimate forms of appropriation.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the farmer, the craftsman, the prospector — all involve substantial transformative labour: clearing and ploughing, carving and shaping, discovering and extracting. The definition extracts the principle that labour grounds ownership, but it draws the threshold of labour too high by requiring transformation. The simplest and most ancient forms of appropriation — gathering, collecting, claiming — involve taking possession of unowned things with minimal labour, and the definition's insistence on transformative labour excludes them, even though the intuition that first possession confers ownership is at least as deep as the intuition that labour does.
passage:
The cases most commonly invoked to illustrate the labour theory of property share a dramatic feature: in each, the owner's effort visibly transforms the object. The farmer converts wilderness into cultivated land. The craftsman converts raw wood into a finished chair. The prospector converts inert ground into extracted ore. The labour is strenuous, the transformation is manifest, and the resulting ownership seems to follow naturally from the effort invested.

One might codify these cases by defining ownership as arising when a person mixes labour with an unowned object, transforming it, while leaving enough for others. This definition captures the paradigm cases admirably. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency involves the most elemental form of appropriation: simple gathering. A person walking along an unowned stretch of coastline collects pieces of driftwood that have washed ashore. She exerts minimal effort — she bends down and picks them up. She has not transformed the driftwood; the tides deposited it in its present condition. Condition (i) is at best marginally satisfied. Yet it seems plain that she has a legitimate claim to the driftwood she has gathered. If another person were to take it from her arms, we should not hesitate to say that something belonging to her had been taken. The intuition here is not grounded in transformative labour but in first possession — in the simple act of taking hold of what belongs to no one. One might observe that the labour theory, by requiring transformation, draws the threshold of ownership-generating activity too high and excludes the ancient and widely shared intuition that first possession — the simple taking of an unowned thing — is itself sufficient to ground a claim, even when the labour involved is negligible.

background_cases: A burglar who breaks into a house intending to steal valuables acts with criminal intent because the burglar consciously chose to enter the premises, formed the specific purpose of taking another's property, and was aware that the act was unlawful.
An arsonist who deliberately sets fire to a building acts with criminal intent because the arsonist planned the act, intended to destroy the property, and was aware that arson is a criminal offence.

A fraudster who forges documents to obtain money acts with criminal intent because the fraudster deliberately created false instruments, intended to deceive, and was aware that forgery and fraud are crimes.
definition: A person P acted with criminal intent in performing act X if and only if (i) P consciously chose to perform X, (ii) P intended to bring about the specific harmful result that X produced, and (iii) P was aware that X was unlawful.
conditions: P consciously chose to perform X
P intended the specific harmful result
P was aware of the unlawfulness of X
counterexample: Consider a motorist who, late for an appointment, drives at excessive speed through a residential area, consciously aware that speeding is unlawful and that it creates a risk of harm to pedestrians, but without any intention or desire to injure anyone. The motorist strikes and seriously injures a child. The motorist consciously chose to speed — condition (i) is met. The motorist was aware that speeding is unlawful — condition (iii) is met. But the motorist did not intend the specific harmful result — the injury of the child. Condition (ii) is not satisfied. The definition therefore pronounces that the motorist lacked criminal intent. Yet the law in most jurisdictions would hold the motorist criminally culpable, on the ground that the motorist consciously disregarded a known and substantial risk of serious harm — a state of mind the law calls recklessness, which the definition cannot accommodate.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the burglar, the arsonist, the fraudster — all involve actors who not only chose their actions and knew them to be unlawful but who specifically intended the harmful outcome: the theft, the destruction, the deception. The definition captures this paradigm of purposeful wrongdoing but cannot accommodate the distinct mental state of recklessness, in which the actor does not intend the harm but consciously disregards a substantial and unjustifiable risk that the harm will occur — a mental state that the criminal law treats as a form of culpable intent.
passage:
The cases most straightforwardly illustrative of criminal intent share a clarity of purpose that the standard definition captures well. The burglar intends to steal. The arsonist intends to burn. The fraudster intends to deceive. In each case, the actor consciously chose the criminal act, formed the specific purpose of producing the harmful result, and was aware of its unlawfulness. The alignment of choice, purpose, and awareness produces a paradigm of criminal culpability that the definition faithfully represents.

One might define criminal intent as the conscious choice to perform an unlawful act with the specific intention of producing the harmful result that the act in fact produces. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this definition involves a form of culpability that is equally important in criminal law but structurally different from the paradigm cases. A motorist, running late, drives at excessive speed through a residential area. She is conscious of her choice to speed. She is aware that speeding is unlawful. She is also aware that driving at excessive speed in a residential area creates a substantial risk of serious injury to pedestrians. But she does not intend to injure anyone — she hopes, indeed expects, that she will arrive safely without incident. She strikes a child. The motorist consciously chose the unlawful act and was aware of the risk, but she did not intend the specific harmful result. Condition (ii) is not satisfied, and the definition pronounces her free of criminal intent. Yet the law would hold her criminally culpable, recognising that her conscious disregard of a known and substantial risk — her recklessness — constitutes a form of culpable mental state that, while falling short of specific intent, is sufficiently blameworthy to ground criminal liability. One might observe that the definition, by requiring specific intent as to the harmful result, captures only the most purposeful form of criminal culpability and excludes recklessness, which occupies a central place in the criminal law's hierarchy of mental states.

background_cases: A merchant who agrees to deliver one hundred bales of cotton by a fixed date, in exchange for payment upon delivery, enters a binding contract because consideration has been exchanged, both parties have assented to the same terms, and the terms are sufficiently definite; the contract is enforceable because the obligations on both sides are clear and reciprocal.

A publisher who agrees to print and distribute an author's manuscript, in exchange for a share of the profits, enters a binding contract because each party has given something of value, both have assented, and the essential terms — the manuscript, the distribution, the profit-sharing arrangement — are definite; the contract is enforceable because the mutual obligations are specified.

A builder who agrees to construct a wall for a landowner at a fixed price enters a binding contract because the builder promises labour and materials, the landowner promises payment, both have assented, and the terms are definite; the contract is enforceable because the reciprocal obligations are clear.
definition: An agreement between parties A and B constitutes an enforceable contract if and only if (i) each party has provided consideration — each has given or promised something of value to the other, (ii) both parties have assented to the same terms, and (iii) the essential terms of the agreement are sufficiently definite to be given legal effect.
conditions: Each party has provided consideration
Both parties have assented to the same terms
The essential terms are sufficiently definite
counterexample: Consider a contract between a shipowner and a crew of sailors, entered into at the beginning of a voyage. The shipowner promises wages; the sailors promise their labour. Both parties assent to the same terms. The essential terms — the voyage, the wages, the duties of the crew — are definite. All three conditions are met. Midway through the voyage, a storm damages the ship and several sailors desert. The remaining sailors, whose labour is now indispensable to the completion of the voyage, refuse to continue unless the shipowner promises to increase their wages. The shipowner, having no alternative — he cannot recruit new crew in mid-ocean — agrees. The sailors provide no new consideration for the increased wages; they are promising to do only what they were already contractually bound to do. Yet the shipowner's promise, extracted under the pressure of the sailors' indispensability, raises the question of whether the modified agreement is enforceable.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve agreements formed in conditions of genuine freedom, where each party has alternatives and the exchange of promises is untainted by the exploitation of situational advantage. The definition captures the formal requirements of contract formation but does not address the circumstances under which an agreement is modified. The sailors' case reveals that a party who extracts a promise by exploiting a position of practical compulsion — the shipowner's inability to find alternative crew in mid-ocean — may secure an agreement that satisfies the definition's formal conditions (if one is generous about consideration) while violating the principle that contractual modification should be freely bargained.
passage:
When one examines the contracts most readily acknowledged as enforceable, a common feature emerges: the parties enter the agreement in conditions of genuine commercial freedom. The cotton merchant is free to sell to another buyer; the publisher is free to decline the manuscript; the builder is free to take another commission. Each party's promise is given against a background of alternatives, and the resulting agreement reflects a genuine meeting of wills rather than the submission of one party to the other's superior position.

One might codify the conditions of an enforceable contract by requiring consideration, mutual assent, and definite terms. This definition captures the formal structure of the paradigmatic cases. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises in the context of contractual modification under pressure. Suppose a shipowner engages a crew of sailors for a voyage. Consideration is exchanged: the shipowner promises wages, the sailors promise their labour. Both parties assent to the same terms, which are sufficiently definite. Midway through the voyage, a storm damages the ship and several sailors desert. The remaining sailors, upon whose labour the completion of the voyage now depends, refuse to continue unless the shipowner promises to double their wages. The shipowner, unable to recruit new crew on the open sea, agrees. The modified agreement appears to satisfy the three conditions: the shipowner promises increased wages, the sailors promise continued service, both assent, and the terms are definite. Yet the sailors' promise is to do no more than they were already bound to do, and the shipowner's promise is extracted under a form of practical compulsion — he has no alternative but to agree. The background cases all involve agreements reached in conditions of freedom; the modified contract is reached under conditions of duress that the definition's formal requirements cannot detect. One might observe that an adequate definition of enforceable contract must attend not only to the formal elements of consideration, assent, and definiteness but also to the conditions under which these elements are obtained.

background_cases: A manufacturer of motor-cars is liable for injuries caused by a defective braking system because the manufacturer placed the product into the stream of commerce, the defect existed at the time of manufacture, and the user was injured in the course of ordinary use; the liability flows from the manufacturer's control over the product's design and production.

A manufacturer of tinned food is liable for illness caused by a contaminated product because the contamination occurred during the manufacturing process, the product reached the consumer in its original sealed condition, and the consumer used the product in the ordinary manner; the liability attaches because the manufacturer was the party best positioned to prevent the defect.

A manufacturer of electrical appliances is liable for injuries caused by defective wiring because the defect was present when the product left the factory, the consumer used the appliance as intended, and the manufacturer had exclusive control over the wiring during production; the liability reflects the manufacturer's unique opportunity to detect and correct the defect.
definition: A manufacturer M is liable for harm caused by a defective product P if and only if (i) P was defective when it left M's control, (ii) the defect caused harm to a consumer who used P in the manner intended, and (iii) M placed P into the stream of commerce with the expectation that it would reach the consumer without substantial alteration.
conditions: P was defective when it left M's control
The defect caused harm during intended use
M placed P into commerce expecting no substantial alteration
counterexample: Consider a manufacturer of industrial chemicals who sells a solvent to a distributor, who then repackages it into smaller containers for retail sale. During repackaging, the distributor uses containers that are chemically incompatible with the solvent, causing a reaction that produces toxic fumes. A consumer who uses the repackaged solvent in the intended manner is injured by the fumes. The solvent was not defective when it left the manufacturer's control — the defect was introduced by the distributor's choice of incompatible containers. The manufacturer placed the product into commerce expecting it to reach consumers without substantial alteration, but the distributor's repackaging constitutes a substantial alteration. Condition (i) is not satisfied, and condition (iii) is defeated by the distributor's intervention. The definition absolves the manufacturer. Yet the manufacturer chose to sell a product that it knew would be repackaged, and it could have specified compatible container materials or warned the distributor of the incompatibility.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve products that reach the consumer in the same condition in which they left the manufacturer — the motor-car with its original braking system, the sealed tin of food, the appliance with its factory wiring. The definition captures the manufacturer's liability in these unbroken chains of custody. Yet the chemical-repackaging case reveals that manufacturers may be implicated in harms caused by foreseeable alterations performed by intermediaries, even when the product was not defective at the point of manufacture. The definition's requirement that the product reach the consumer without substantial alteration does not account for the manufacturer's responsibility to anticipate and guard against foreseeable downstream modifications of its product.
passage:
The cases most straightforwardly cited as instances of manufacturer liability share a structural feature: the product that causes harm is the same product, in the same condition, that the manufacturer placed into commerce. The motor-car's defective braking system was installed at the factory. The contaminated tin of food was sealed at the cannery. The appliance's faulty wiring was soldered during assembly. In each case, the chain of custody is unbroken, and the manufacturer's liability flows from its exclusive control over the product at the point of origin.

One might codify this pattern by defining manufacturer liability as arising when a product was defective at the point of manufacture, caused harm during intended use, and reached the consumer without substantial alteration. This definition captures the paradigmatic cases. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when a foreseeable intermediate alteration introduces the defect. Suppose a manufacturer of industrial chemicals sells a solvent in bulk to a distributor, who repackages it into smaller retail containers. The distributor, unaware of the chemical properties of the solvent, uses containers made of a material that reacts with the solvent to produce toxic fumes. A consumer is injured. The solvent was not defective when it left the manufacturer's control, and the distributor's repackaging constitutes a substantial alteration. The definition absolves the manufacturer. Yet the manufacturer knew that its product would be repackaged — bulk-to-retail distribution is standard practice in the chemical industry — and it could have specified compatible container materials, warned the distributor, or designed the solvent to be chemically inert with common container materials. The background cases all involve unbroken chains of custody; the repackaging case involves a foreseeable alteration that the manufacturer could have anticipated and prevented. One might observe that the definition, by requiring that the product reach the consumer unaltered, takes no account of the manufacturer's responsibility for foreseeable alterations in the distribution chain, and thereby absolves manufacturers who have failed to guard against dangers that their own commercial practices make probable.

background_cases: A surgeon who leaves a surgical instrument inside a patient's body is negligent because the standard of care requires that instruments be counted before and after surgery, the omission of this routine precaution falls below the professional standard, and the patient suffers harm as a direct result; the negligence consists in the failure to observe a well-established protocol.

A pharmacist who dispenses the wrong medication is negligent because the standard of care requires careful verification of prescriptions, the error falls below what a competent pharmacist would do, and the patient is harmed; the negligence consists in the failure to exercise the care that the profession demands.

An engineer who designs a bridge without adequate load calculations is negligent because the standard of care requires thorough structural analysis, the omission falls below professional standards, and the bridge's users are endangered; the negligence consists in the failure to perform a calculation that any competent engineer would perform.
definition: A professional P is negligent in the performance of duties if and only if (i) P owed a duty of care to the person harmed, (ii) P's conduct fell below the standard of care exercised by competent members of the same profession, and (iii) the person harmed suffered injury as a proximate result of P's substandard conduct.
conditions: P owed a duty of care
P's conduct fell below the professional standard
Injury resulted as a proximate consequence
counterexample: Consider a physician who, following the accepted medical guidelines of her time, prescribes a medication that is later discovered to cause serious side-effects in a small percentage of patients. At the time of prescription, the medication was regarded as safe by the entire medical profession; the guidelines recommended it for the patient's condition; and no competent physician would have done differently. The patient suffers a serious adverse reaction. The physician owed a duty of care, and the patient suffered injury. But the physician's conduct did not fall below the professional standard — it met the standard precisely. Condition (ii) is not satisfied, and the definition correctly absolves her. Yet one might observe that the patient's injury is real and was caused by the physician's treatment, and that the professional standard itself was defective — the guidelines were wrong. The patient has suffered harm through no fault of her own, and the definition, by measuring negligence against a professional standard that may itself be flawed, provides no remedy.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve departures from professional standards that were themselves sound: counting surgical instruments is a reliable precaution, verifying prescriptions is a reliable safeguard, and calculating structural loads is a reliable method. The standard of care, in these cases, is a trustworthy benchmark. The definition measures negligence against this benchmark. Yet the medication case reveals that the professional standard itself may be inadequate — the guidelines may be wrong, the accepted practice may be harmful. When the standard is defective, a professional who adheres to it is not negligent by the definition's measure, yet the patient is harmed. The definition assumes that the professional standard is a reliable proxy for reasonable care, and when that assumption fails, the definition cannot identify the source of the harm.
passage:
When one examines the cases most commonly cited as instances of professional negligence, a shared assumption emerges: the professional standard of care is taken to be a reliable measure of what constitutes reasonable conduct. The surgeon who leaves an instrument in a patient has failed to count instruments — a protocol that, when followed, prevents this very harm. The pharmacist who dispenses the wrong medication has failed to verify the prescription — a procedure that, when followed, catches errors. The engineer who omits load calculations has failed to perform an analysis that, when performed, ensures structural safety. In each case, the professional standard is sound, and departure from it is culpable.

One might codify this pattern by defining negligence as conduct that falls below the standard of a competent professional, resulting in harm to a person to whom a duty of care is owed. This definition captures the paradigmatic cases. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when the professional standard itself is defective. Suppose a physician prescribes a medication in strict accordance with the accepted guidelines of her profession. The medication is recommended for the patient's condition by every authoritative body; no competent physician would have prescribed differently. The patient suffers a serious adverse reaction — a side-effect that was not recognised at the time because the clinical trials upon which the guidelines were based were inadequate. The physician owed a duty of care. The patient suffered harm. But the physician's conduct met the professional standard precisely; she did what every competent colleague would have done. Condition (ii) is not satisfied, and the definition absolves her. Yet the patient's injury is real and was caused by the physician's treatment. The background cases all involve departures from a sound standard; this case involves perfect adherence to a defective one. One might observe that the definition, by measuring negligence against the prevailing professional standard, assumes that the standard is itself adequate, and that when the standard is flawed, the definition cannot identify the harm that flows from universal adherence to a rule that ought to have been different.

background_cases: In the classic case of Carlill v Carbolic Smoke Ball Co, the company's advertisement promising a reward to anyone who used its product and contracted influenza was held to be an offer to the world at large, accepted by performance; the court found consideration in the detriment suffered by the claimant in using the product as directed.

In the formation of a simple sale of goods, the buyer's promise to pay the price is consideration for the seller's promise to deliver, and vice versa; each party's undertaking is the price paid for the other's, and the reciprocity of obligation constitutes the essence of the bargain.

In the case of a unilateral contract for the offer of a reward — such as a reward for the return of a lost dog — the offeror's promise is supported by the finder's act of returning the dog; the act constitutes both acceptance of the offer and the consideration that makes the promise enforceable.
definition: A promise is enforceable as a contract if and only if (i) the promise is supported by consideration — that is, something of value given in exchange, (ii) the parties have reached agreement through offer and acceptance, and (iii) the consideration moves from the promisee to the promisor.
conditions: The promise is supported by consideration
The parties have reached agreement through offer and acceptance
The consideration moves from the promisee to the promisor
counterexample: Consider a charitable pledge in which a wealthy benefactor promises to donate a large sum to a hospital, and the hospital, in reliance upon the pledge, undertakes expensive construction of a new wing. The benefactor later refuses to pay. No consideration in the traditional sense has moved from the hospital to the benefactor — the hospital has given nothing of value to the benefactor in exchange for the promise. Conditions (i) and (iii) appear to fail. Yet one might observe that the hospital has suffered a detriment in reliance upon the promise, and that justice demands the promise be enforced.
abductive_insight: The background cases share the feature that consideration is part of a reciprocal bargain: the buyer pays for the goods, the user of the smoke ball suffers the detriment of using the product, the finder expends effort in returning the dog. The charitable pledge lacks reciprocal bargain but possesses a different ground of enforceability: the promisee's detrimental reliance. What the definition captures is the bargain model of consideration, but the charitable case reveals that enforceability may rest upon reliance rather than exchange. The definition, by insisting upon consideration moving from promisee to promisor, cannot accommodate promissory estoppel — the principle that a promise may be enforced when the promisee has relied upon it to his detriment.
passage:
The doctrine of consideration is central to the common law of contract. In Carlill v Carbolic Smoke Ball Co, the claimant's detriment in using the smoke ball constituted consideration for the company's promise. In the ordinary sale of goods, each party's promise is the consideration for the other's. In the reward case, the finder's act of returning the lost property is both acceptance and consideration. In each paradigmatic case, something of value passes from the promisee to the promisor as part of a reciprocal bargain.

One might therefore define an enforceable promise as one supported by consideration moving from the promisee to the promisor, where the parties have reached agreement through offer and acceptance. This definition captures the bargain theory of consideration that the common law has long favoured. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises with charitable pledges. Suppose a wealthy benefactor promises to donate a substantial sum to a hospital. The hospital, in reliance upon this pledge, enters into contracts for the construction of a new wing, incurring significant expense. The benefactor subsequently declines to honour his pledge. No consideration in the traditional sense has moved from the hospital to the benefactor — the hospital has given nothing in exchange for the promise. The definition would deny enforceability. Yet the hospital has altered its position in reliance upon the promise and will suffer serious detriment if the promise is not kept. What the background cases possess is reciprocal exchange, but the charitable case reveals a different ground of enforceability: detrimental reliance. The definition, by insisting upon the movement of consideration as part of a bargain, cannot accommodate the principle that a promise inducing reasonable reliance ought to be enforceable even in the absence of a reciprocal exchange.

background_cases: In a straightforward road accident, the defendant's careless driving is the cause of the claimant's injuries: but for the defendant's negligence, the claimant would not have been harmed, and the negligence was the proximate cause, with no intervening events breaking the chain.

In a medical negligence case, a surgeon's failure to perform a routine check leads directly to a complication that causes the patient's condition to worsen; the chain of causation runs unbroken from the omission to the harm.

In a product liability case, a defective brake pad causes a vehicle to fail to stop, and the vehicle collides with a wall; the defect is both the but-for cause and the proximate cause, with no independent intervening factor.
definition: An act A is the legal cause of a harm H if and only if (i) H would not have occurred but for A, (ii) A was sufficiently proximate to H that a reasonable person would regard A as a cause of H, and (iii) no act of a third party intervened between A and H.
conditions: H would not have occurred but for A
A was sufficiently proximate to H that a reasonable person would regard A as a cause of H
No act of a third party intervened between A and H
counterexample: Consider a negligent driver who causes a minor collision, injuring a pedestrian. An ambulance is called. On the way to hospital, a second driver negligently runs a red signal and collides with the ambulance, aggravating the pedestrian's injuries severely. The original driver's negligence is a but-for cause of the aggravated injuries — but for the first accident, the pedestrian would not have been in the ambulance. Yet a third party (the second driver) has intervened. Condition (iii) fails. The definition would deny that the first driver's negligence caused the aggravated harm, yet one might observe that the ambulance journey was a foreseeable consequence of the initial accident.
abductive_insight: The background cases share the feature that the causal chain runs directly from the defendant's act to the harm without interruption by an independent agent. The ambulance case introduces a third-party intervention, but crucially, the intervention occurs within a chain of events that was set in motion by, and was a foreseeable consequence of, the original negligence. What the definition's third condition fails to capture is the distinction between intervening acts that are foreseeable consequences of the defendant's conduct and those that are genuinely independent and unforeseeable. The paradigmatic cases lack intervening acts entirely, but legal causation can survive an intervention when the intervention was a natural and probable consequence of the original wrong.
passage:
In the paradigmatic cases of legal causation, the chain from wrongful act to harm runs unbroken. The careless driver collides with the pedestrian. The negligent surgeon's omission leads directly to the patient's deterioration. The defective brake pad causes the vehicle to strike the wall. In each case, no independent agent intervenes between the defendant's conduct and the claimant's injury.

One might codify this pattern by defining legal causation as follows: an act causes a harm if the harm would not have occurred but for the act, the act is sufficiently proximate, and no third-party act intervenes. This definition captures the structure of the paradigmatic cases. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when a foreseeable chain of events includes a third-party intervention. Suppose a negligent driver injures a pedestrian in a collision. An ambulance is summoned — a perfectly foreseeable response to the accident. On the journey to hospital, a second negligent driver strikes the ambulance, severely aggravating the pedestrian's injuries. The first driver's negligence is a but-for cause of the aggravated harm: but for the initial collision, the pedestrian would not have been in the ambulance. The harm is proximate in the sense that a reasonable person would trace the sequence back to the first accident. Yet a third party has intervened. The definition would deny causation. What the background cases and the ambulance case share is that the sequence of events following the defendant's act was foreseeable. What distinguishes them is only the presence of an independent actor in the chain. It appears that the definition, by absolutely excluding cases with third-party intervention, cannot accommodate the well-established legal principle that an intervening act does not break the chain of causation when it is a natural and probable consequence of the original wrong.

background_cases: In Donoghue v Stevenson, the manufacturer of ginger beer was held liable to the ultimate consumer for injuries caused by a decomposed snail in the bottle, even though no contract existed between them; the House of Lords held that the manufacturer owed a duty of care to persons whom he could reasonably foresee would be affected by his negligence.

In the case of a negligently manufactured motor vehicle, the manufacturer is held liable to any person injured by the defect, whether the buyer, a passenger, or a bystander, on the ground that all such persons are within the foreseeable range of risk created by the defective product.

In the case of a pharmacist who negligently compounds a prescription, liability extends to the patient who takes the medicine, even though the pharmacist's contractual relationship is with the prescribing physician; the patient is within the foreseeable class of persons endangered by the error.
definition: A manufacturer M is liable for harm caused by a defective product if and only if (i) the product was defective when it left M's control, (ii) the claimant was in a contractual relationship with M, and (iii) the defect was the cause of the harm.
conditions: The product was defective when it left M's control
The claimant was in a contractual relationship with M
The defect was the cause of the harm
counterexample: Consider a consumer who purchases a bottle of lemonade from a retailer. The bottle, manufactured by a company with which the consumer has no contractual relationship, contains a foreign object that causes the consumer to fall ill. The product was defective when it left the manufacturer's control, and the defect caused the harm. Conditions (i) and (iii) are satisfied. Yet the consumer has no contractual relationship with the manufacturer — the contract is with the retailer. The definition would deny liability.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the ginger beer, the motor vehicle, the compounded prescription — all share a common feature: the manufacturer's liability extends beyond the contractual chain to encompass any person whom the manufacturer could reasonably foresee would be harmed by the defect. What grounds liability in these cases is not the presence of a contract but the foreseeability of harm to the class of persons who come into contact with the product. The definition, by requiring privity of contract, reflects a doctrinal position that was decisively abandoned in Donoghue v Stevenson and subsequent developments. The true principle is that a manufacturer who puts a defective product into circulation owes a duty to all foreseeable victims, not merely to his contractual counterpart.
passage:
The development of product liability in the common law reveals a movement away from contractual privity toward a broader principle of responsibility. In Donoghue v Stevenson, the House of Lords held that the manufacturer of ginger beer owed a duty of care to the ultimate consumer, with whom no contract existed. In motor vehicle cases, the manufacturer's liability extends to passengers and bystanders. In pharmaceutical cases, the pharmacist's duty reaches the patient, not merely the prescribing physician. In each case, liability rests upon the foreseeability of harm, not upon the existence of a contractual relationship.

One might nevertheless propose that a manufacturer is liable for a defective product when the product was defective at the time it left the manufacturer's control, the claimant was in a contractual relationship with the manufacturer, and the defect caused the harm. This definition captures the nineteenth-century position, under which the absence of privity barred the consumer's claim against the manufacturer. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is readily at hand. A consumer purchases a bottle of lemonade from a retailer. The bottle, manufactured by a company with whom the consumer has no contractual relationship, contains a foreign object that makes the consumer ill. The product was defective. The defect caused the harm. But the consumer's contract is with the retailer, not the manufacturer. The definition denies liability. What the background cases reveal is that the true ground of the manufacturer's liability is not contractual privity but the foreseeability of harm to persons who use or are affected by the product. The definition, by insisting upon a contractual relationship, cannot capture the modern principle that a manufacturer owes a duty of care to all those who may foreseeably be harmed by his product's defects.

background_cases: A merchant offers to sell one hundred bales of cotton to a buyer at an agreed price; the buyer accepts; both parties intend to be legally bound; the merchant delivers the cotton and the buyer pays. We recognise this as a binding contract because the essential elements — offer, acceptance, intention, and consideration — are present.

A builder agrees with a landowner to construct a wall for a fixed sum; the landowner promises to pay upon completion; the builder completes the wall and the landowner pays. Again, we recognise a binding contract supported by mutual promises.
A tailor agrees to make a suit for a customer at a stated price; the customer promises to pay when the suit is ready; both intend to be bound. The elements of a valid contract are present.
definition: An agreement constitutes a binding contract if and only if (i) one party has made an offer that the other has accepted, (ii) both parties intend to create legal relations, and (iii) the agreement is supported by consideration moving from each party.
conditions: One party has made an offer that the other has accepted
Both parties intend to create legal relations
The agreement is supported by consideration moving from each party
counterexample: A wealthy uncle promises his nephew that he will pay the nephew five thousand pounds on the nephew's twenty-first birthday. The nephew, relying upon the promise, declines a position of employment that he would otherwise have accepted. The uncle later refuses to pay. There is a clear offer (the promise of money), acceptance (the nephew's reliance), and intention to create a legal obligation. Yet no consideration moves from the nephew to the uncle — the nephew has given nothing in exchange for the promise. Condition (iii) is met by the uncle (who promised money) but not by the nephew (who promised nothing in return). The definition would deny contractual force to the arrangement, yet one might observe that the nephew's detrimental reliance creates an injustice that the law ought to address.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved bilateral exchanges: cotton for money, building for payment, tailoring for a fee.
