Yet the seller is a minor of fifteen years, and the applicable law withholds contractual capacity from persons below the age of majority except in respect of necessaries. The agreement satisfies all three inferred conditions and yet is not binding, for it is voidable at the minor's instance. One might observe that every case from which the generalisation was drawn involved parties of full capacity, and that this feature, being universal and uncontested in the sample, was never isolated as a separate condition. We are thus led to recognise a characteristic limitation of abductive reasoning from well-behaved cases: conditions that are uniformly satisfied in the background data may be mistaken for redundancies when they are in fact essential prerequisites, their necessity becoming apparent only when a case arises in which they fail.

background_cases: A farmer who encloses and cultivates a tract of unclaimed wilderness for twenty years acquires title by adverse possession; his sustained, open, and exclusive use of the land is treated as establishing ownership.
A squatter who occupies an abandoned building, maintains it, and pays the property taxes for the statutory period acquires possessory title; the law rewards continuous and responsible occupation.

A rancher who fences and grazes a parcel of land belonging to an absent owner for the full limitation period gains title by prescription; the law treats long and undisturbed possession as equivalent to ownership.
definition: A person acquires ownership of land through long possession if and only if (i) the person has occupied the land continuously for the statutory period, (ii) the possession has been open and notorious, and (iii) the possession has been exclusive.
conditions: The person has occupied the land continuously for the statutory period
The possession has been open and notorious
The possession has been exclusive
counterexample: Suppose a person occupies a parcel of land continuously, openly, and exclusively for the full statutory period, but does so with the express permission of the true owner, who has granted a revocable licence to use the land. All three inferred conditions are satisfied, yet in virtually every jurisdiction the claim of adverse possession will fail, for possession by permission of the owner is not adverse. The occupier's tenure, however long, open, and exclusive, does not ripen into ownership because it was never hostile to the owner's title.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved possessors who occupied land without the owner's consent — the farmer on unclaimed land, the squatter in an abandoned building, the rancher on an absent owner's property. Hostility to the true owner's title was present in every case but was not identified as a separate condition because it was never absent in the sample. The licensed occupier demonstrates that the requirement of adverse or hostile possession is essential and cannot be deduced from continuity, openness, and exclusivity alone. The abductive inference, drawn from uniformly hostile possessions, omitted the very feature that distinguished its examples from mere permissive occupation.
passage:
The doctrine of adverse possession permits a person who has occupied another's land for a sufficiently long period to acquire title, provided that certain conditions are met. When one examines the standard cases — the farmer who encloses wilderness, the squatter who maintains an abandoned dwelling, the rancher who fences a neglected parcel — one finds that continuous, open, and exclusive possession for the statutory period appears to be both necessary and sufficient. From these cases, an abductive inference might yield a definition consisting of precisely these three conditions.

Yet the inference overlooks a feature that was tacitly present in every background case. In each instance, the possessor occupied the land without the consent of the true owner: the farmer settled unclaimed territory, the squatter entered an abandoned building, and the rancher enclosed a parcel whose owner had departed. Now suppose a person who occupies land continuously, openly, and exclusively for the full statutory period, but who does so under an express licence from the true owner. Every stated condition is satisfied, yet the claim of adverse possession will fail, for the law requires that possession be hostile — that is, without the owner's permission. One might observe that hostility was so uniformly present in the paradigm cases that it was invisible as a separate requirement. The licensed occupier's case reveals that the inductive generalisation, by drawing only upon instances of uninvited possession, incorporated hostility as a background assumption rather than as an explicit condition, and its insufficiency becomes apparent only when a cooperative possessor satisfies all the stated criteria without establishing the adversity that the law demands.

background_cases: A motorist who fails to check his mirrors before changing lanes and collides with another vehicle is held liable in negligence; his inattention fell below the standard of reasonable care.
A shop owner who neglects to mop a wet floor, causing a customer to slip and fracture her wrist, is held liable; the failure to maintain safe premises constituted a breach of duty.
A surgeon who leaves a swab inside a patient's abdomen during an operation is held liable in negligence; the error falls below the standard expected of a competent practitioner.
definition: A defendant is liable in negligence if and only if (i) the defendant owed a duty of care to the claimant, (ii) the defendant's conduct fell below the standard of reasonable care, and (iii) the claimant suffered physical injury as a direct consequence.
conditions: The defendant owed a duty of care to the claimant
The defendant's conduct fell below the standard of reasonable care
The claimant suffered physical injury as a direct consequence
counterexample: Suppose an accountant negligently prepares a financial statement that materially overstates a company's assets. A prospective investor, to whom the accountant owes a duty of care, relies upon the statement and invests a substantial sum, suffering severe financial loss when the company's true position is revealed. The accountant owed a duty, breached it, and the investor suffered loss directly as a consequence. Yet the loss is purely financial, not physical. The inferred definition, which requires physical injury, would deny liability, though the law in many jurisdictions recognises claims for pure economic loss caused by negligent misstatement.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved physical harm — a traffic collision, a fracture from a fall, a retained surgical swab. The inferred definition accordingly required physical injury as a necessary element of negligence liability. But this requirement reflects the composition of the sample rather than a genuine limitation of the law. The accountant's case demonstrates that negligence liability can attach to pure economic loss, particularly in cases of negligent misstatement where the defendant assumed a responsibility for the accuracy of the information provided. The abductive inference, confined to cases of physical injury, failed to apprehend the broader reach of the duty of care.
passage:
The most familiar cases of negligence liability involve physical harm: the careless motorist who causes a collision, the inattentive shopkeeper whose wet floor injures a customer, the surgeon who leaves an instrument inside a patient. From these cases, one might reasonably infer that liability in negligence requires a duty of care, a breach of the standard of reasonable care, and physical injury suffered as a direct consequence of the breach.

Yet this inference draws its third condition from the particular character of the background cases rather than from the structure of the tort itself. Suppose an accountant who, in preparing a financial statement, negligently overstates a company's assets. A prospective investor, to whom the accountant owes a recognised duty of care, relies upon the statement, commits a large sum, and suffers substantial financial loss when the true position emerges. The duty is owed, the breach is clear, and the loss follows directly. But the injury is purely financial. The inferred definition would deny recovery, yet the law in a number of jurisdictions permits it, recognising that a person who assumes responsibility for the accuracy of information may be held liable for economic loss caused by negligent misstatement. One should observe that every background case happened to involve bodily harm, and the generalisation mistook this common feature for a necessary element. The accountant's case reveals that the reach of negligence extends beyond physical injury, and that an abductive inference confined to one category of harm will produce a definition that is both too narrow and descriptively inaccurate.
